blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ III
LEFSCHETZ FORMULAby A. Grothendieck, written up by L. Illusie

In this exposé, we prove the Lefschetz formula in étale cohomology, for correspondences between complexes of sheaves, in Verdier's formulation (cf. [18]). In an earlier write-up, distributed by the IHES about ten years ago, the formula was stated, under resolution hypotheses, but the required compatibilities were not proved. Deligne's finiteness theorems (SGA 41/2 Finitude) have made it possible to dispose of these hypotheses, provided one works with schemes separated and of finite type over a field. As for the compatibilities, the redactor has (painstakingly) verified them.

Let us say right away that the Lefschetz formula in question here, just like the trace formula for constant coefficients (SGA 41/2 Cycle), which it generalizes, is essentially trivial: it merely expresses certain compatibilities in the duality formalism. Roughly speaking, it says that, under certain conditions, the trace of an endomorphism of a hypercohomology complex \(R\Gamma(X,L)\), where \(L\) is a complex of sheaves on \(X\), can be computed as the "integral" of certain cohomology classes on \(X\times X\). In practice, the endomorphisms one considers are associated with correspondences \(C\subset X\times X\), and the classes one recovers on the product have support in \(C\cap\Delta\), where \(\Delta\) is the diagonal; these classes are "of a local nature" in a neighborhood of the fixed points, which, in principle, facilitates their computation. See 4.2, 4.7 for precise statements. In fact, the computation of these "local" classes in terms of more comprehensible invariants, without which the present formula would have no interest, has so far been undertaken only in very particular situations (see 4.10, 4.11, and the following exposé, where the case of the Frobenius correspondence on a curve is treated in particular).

Lacking a good derived category of \(\ell\)-adic sheaves (Jouanolou's thesis unfortunately not having been published), we work systematically with torsion coefficients (prime to the residue characteristics). In spite of minor technical difficulties in the definition of traces, this point of view in fact leads to finer formulas, which are also more manageable insofar as, for the computation of the local terms, one wishes to trivialize the sheaves involved by coverings (see in particular (SGA 41/2 Rapport), III B, and XII).

In no 1, we fix the notation and recall various Künneth formulas from (SGA 41/2 Finitude), which play an essential role in what follows. We also introduce a certain number of fibered and cofibered categories, whose consideration facilitates certain compatibility verifications later on. No 2, very technical, may be skipped on first reading. Its main result (2.5) expresses a compatibility between the external tensor product of \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}\), direct image and base change. In no 3, we define cohomological correspondences between complexes, and show how they act on hypercohomology; the result cited above makes it possible to describe this action in various equivalent ways. The heart of the exposé is no 4, where the Verdier pairing between cohomological correspondences is defined, and the Lefschetz formula proved, in a relative situation appreciably more general than the one usually considered, with a view to later applications to computations of local terms. We give the most obvious corollaries for correspondences with constant coefficients. In no 5, we indicate briefly how to generalize to correspondences between complexes certain usual constructions on correspondences with constant coefficients (cf. [12]), such as passage to the dual, composition, external tensor product. Most of the verifications, analogous to those of the preceding sections, have been omitted. Finally, in an appendix, we paraphrase the preceding theory in the context of coherent sheaves, using the duality formalism of [8]. Here one can work over an arbitrary noetherian base \(S\), provided one makes, when necessary, hypotheses of properness, of finite tor-dimension and of tor-independence on the \(S\)-schemes considered. We show that the very general Lefschetz formula one obtains contains as a special case the Woods Hole formula [1], and point out some applications.
CONTENTS.

2.Functoriality of \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}\) and external tensor products9
3.Cohomological correspondences17
4.Pairings of correspondences. Lefschetz formula27
5.Complements41
6.Appendix. Lefschetz formula for coherent sheaves48

pages.title: 1. Notation and review of Kunneth formulas.

blocks.html: 1. Notation and review of Kunneth formulas.
1.1 We denote by \(S\) the spectrum of a field. Unless otherwise stated, the \(S\)-schemes considered in the sequel will be \(S\)-schemes separated and of finite type.

We denote by \(A\) a commutative noetherian ring, annihilated by an integer invertible on \(S\). If \(X\) is an \(S\)-scheme, we denote by \(D(X)=D(X,A)\) the derived category of complexes of \(A\)-Modules on \(X\) (for the étale topology), and by \(D_{ctf}(X)\) the full subcategory consisting of the complexes of finite tor-dimension, with constructible cohomology. The category \(D_{ctf}(X)\) is a triangulated subcategory of \(D(X)\). If \(E,F\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(X)\), it follows easily from the definitions that \(E\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}F\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(X)\). By (SGA 41/2 Th. Finitude 1.6, 1.7), we also have \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F)\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(X)\).
1.2 Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be an \(S\)-morphism. We trivially have
\[ f^*D_{ctf}(Y)\subset D_{ctf}(X), \]
and, by (loc. cit.),
\[ \begin{aligned} Rf_*D_{ctf}(X)&\subset D_{ctf}(Y),\\ Rf_!D_{ctf}(X)&\subset D_{ctf}(Y),\\ Rf^!D_{ctf}(Y)&\subset D_{ctf}(X), \end{aligned} \]

(the inclusion relative to \(Rf_!\) uses only the finiteness theorem for proper morphisms (SGA 4 XIV) and the sorite (SGA 4 XVII 5.2.11), so it holds more generally for any compactifiable morphism with fibers of bounded dimension). In the sequel, we shall sometimes write \(f_*\), \(f_!\), \(f^!\) instead of \(Rf_*\), \(Rf_!\), \(Rf^!\); the hypotheses of 1.1 imply that these functors are defined on the entire derived category.
1.3 For \(f:X\longrightarrow S\), we shall set

and denote by \(D_X\) (or \(D\)) the functor \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(-,K_X)\). By (SGA 41/2 Th. Finitude 4.3), the complex \(K_X\) is dualizing: for \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(X)\), we have canonically
\[ E\xrightarrow{\sim}DDE. \]
1.4 Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be an \(S\)-morphism, \(L\in\operatorname{ob}D(X)\), \(M\in\operatorname{ob}D(Y)\). We shall set

\[ \begin{aligned} \operatorname{Hom}^{(1)}_f(L,M)&=\operatorname{Hom}(f^*M,L) \quad\bigl(\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Hom}(M,f_*L)\bigr),\\ \operatorname{Hom}^{(2)}_f(L,M)&=\operatorname{Hom}(L,f^*M),\\ \operatorname{Hom}^{(3)}_f(L,M)&=\operatorname{Hom}(f_!L,M) \quad\bigl(\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Hom}(L,f^!M)\bigr),\qquad\text{(SGA 4 XVIII 3.1.5)}\\ \operatorname{Hom}^{(4)}_f(L,M)&=\operatorname{Hom}(f^!M,L). \end{aligned} \]

The elements of \(\operatorname{Hom}^{(i)}_f(L,M)\) will be called arrows of type \(i\) from \(L\) to \(M\) above \(f\) (or \(f\)-arrows of type \(i\) from \(L\) to \(M\)). The transitivity isomorphisms for the functors \(f^*\), \(f_*\), \(f_!\), \(f^!\) make it possible to compose arrows of type \(i\) for fixed \(i\), and one thus obtains certain fibered or cofibered categories over the category of \(S\)-schemes: for example, for \(i=1\) (resp. \(i=3\)) one obtains the fibered and cofibered category considered in (SGA 4 XVII 4.1.3) (resp. (SGA 4 XVIII 3.1.13)), whose fiber at \(X\) is the category opposite to \(D(X)\) (resp. \(D(X)\)).
1.5 Let, for \(i=1,2\), \(X_i\) be an \(S\)-scheme, \(X=X_1\times_S X_2\), \(L_i\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(X_i)\). We shall set
\[ L_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S L_2 =\operatorname{pr}_1^*L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}\operatorname{pr}_2^*L_2, \]
where \(\operatorname{pr}_i:X\longrightarrow X_i\) is the canonical projection.
1.6 Let, for \(i=1,2\), \(f_i:X_i\longrightarrow Y_i\) be an \(S\)-morphism,
\[ f=f_1\times_Sf_2:X=X_1\times_SX_2\longrightarrow Y=Y_1\times_SY_2, \qquad L_i\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(X_i),\quad M_i\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(Y_i). \]
The obvious canonical isomorphism
\[ f_1^*M_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S f_2^*M_2 \xrightarrow{\sim}f^*(M_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S M_2) \tag{1.6.1} \]
makes it possible to define, for \(u_i\in\operatorname{Hom}^{(1)}_{f_i}(L_i,M_i)\) (resp. \(\operatorname{Hom}^{(2)}_{f_i}(L_i,M_i)\)),
\[ u_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S u_2\in \operatorname{Hom}^{(1)}_f(L,M)\quad \text{(resp. }\operatorname{Hom}^{(2)}_f(L,M)\text{)}, \tag{1.6.2} \]
where \(L=L_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S L_2\), \(M=M_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S M_2\). If \(u_i\), \(v_i\) are composable, we have
\[ (v_1u_1)\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S(v_2u_2) =(v_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S v_2) (u_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S u_2). \tag{1.6.3} \]
Taking \(M_i=f_{i*}L_i\), and \(u_i\) of type 1, given by the identity of \(f_{i*}L_i\), 1.6.2 provides a Kunneth arrow (cf. (SGA 4 XVII 5.4.1.4))
\[ f_{1*}L_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S f_{2*}L_2 \longrightarrow f_*(L_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S L_2). \tag{1.6.4} \]

By (SGA 41/2 Th. Finitude 1.9), 1.6.4 is an isomorphism: by 1.6.3 we reduce indeed to the case where \(X_2=Y_2\), \(f_2=1\), and apply (loc. cit. App. ). When \(f_1\) and \(f_2\) are proper, it is not necessary to invoke (loc. cit.): 1.6.4 is a special case of the Kunneth isomorphism (SGA 4 XVII 5.4.3).
1.7 Let us choose a compactification \(f_i=p_ij_i\), where \(j_i\) is an open immersion, \(p_i\) a proper morphism. Composing the obvious canonical isomorphism (where \(j=j_1\times_Sj_2\))
\[ j_{1!}L_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S j_{2!}L_2 \xrightarrow{\sim}j_!(L_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S L_2) \]
with the isomorphism 1.6.4 relative to \((p_i,j_{i!}L_i)\), we obtain a Kunneth isomorphism (SGA 4 XVII 5.4.3)
\[ f_{1!}L_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S f_{2!}L_2 \xrightarrow{\sim}f_!(L_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S L_2). \tag{1.7.1} \]

One easily checks (loc. cit.) that 1.7.1 does not depend on the compactifications chosen, and is compatible with the transitivity isomorphisms for composites \(g_if_i\) (cf. (loc. cit. 5.2.4)). Thanks to 1.7.1, one can define, for \(u_i\in\operatorname{Hom}^{(3)}_{f_i}(L_i,M_i)\)
\[ u_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S u_2\in \operatorname{Hom}^{(3)}_f(L,M) \tag{1.7.2} \]
with \(L\) and \(M\) as in 1.6.2, and one has a transitivity formula analogous to 1.6.3.
For \(L_i=f_i^!M_i\), and \(u_i\) given by the identity of \(L_i\), 1.7.2 provides a Kunneth arrow
\[ f_1^!M_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S f_2^!M_2 \longrightarrow f^!(M_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S M_2). \tag{1.7.3} \]

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.7.4 — The arrow 1.7.3 is an isomorphism.

By the transitivity formula, it suffices to treat the case where \(X_2=Y_2\), \(f_2=1\). By dévissage, we reduce to \(M_2=g_*j_!A_V\), where \(j:V\longrightarrow U\) is an open immersion and \(g:U\longrightarrow Y_2\) a finite morphism, then, by (SGA 4 XVIII 3.1.12.3), to the case where \(M_2=A_{Y_2}\). By localization on \(X_2\), we may assume on the other hand that \(f_1=g_1j_1\), where \(j_1\) is a closed immersion, and \(g_1\) a smooth morphism purely of relative dimension \(n_1\). By transitivity, it suffices to treat the cases

a) \(f_1=g_1\)
b) \(f_1=j_1\).

In case a), the trace morphism provides by adjunction (SGA 4 XVIII 3.2.5) an isomorphism \(f_1^*M_1(n_1)[2n_1]\xrightarrow{\sim}f_1^!M_1\). The latter is compatible with the base change \(Y_2\longrightarrow S\), since this is so for the trace morphism (SGA 4 XVIII 2.9), whence 1.7.4 in this case. In case b), if \(i_1\) denotes the inclusion of the complementary open subset, we have an exact sequence

\[ 0\longrightarrow j_{1*}Rj_1^!M_1\longrightarrow M_1 \longrightarrow Ri_{1*}i_1^*M_1\longrightarrow0, \]

and the compatibility of \(Ri_{1*}\) with base change, a special case of 1.6.4, yields the conclusion.
Thanks to 1.7.3, one can define, for \(u_i\in\operatorname{Hom}^{(4)}_{f_i}(L_i,M_i)\),
\[ u_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S u_2\in \operatorname{Hom}^{(4)}_f(L,M) \tag{1.7.5} \]
(\(L\) and \(M\) as in 1.6.2), with a transitivity formula analogous to 1.6.3.
On the other hand, with the notation of 1.3, 1.7.3 provides an isomorphism
\[ K_{X_1}\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S K_{X_2} \xrightarrow{\sim}K_X. \tag{1.7.6} \]

1.8 Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a \(S\)-morphism, \(L\in\operatorname{ob}D(X)\), \(M\in\operatorname{ob}D(Y)\), \(S'\) an \(S\)-scheme, \(P\in\operatorname{ob}D(S')\), and \(f':X'\longrightarrow Y'\) the morphism deduced from \(f\) by the base change \(S'\longrightarrow S\). The identity arrow of \(P\) can be regarded indifferently as an arrow of type \(i\) (\(1\leq i\leq4\)) above the identity arrow of \(S'\): for \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}^{(i)}_f(L,M)\) (\(1\leq i\leq4\)), one may therefore, by 1.6.2, 1.7.2, 1.7.5, consider
\[ u\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S\operatorname{Id}_P \in\operatorname{Hom}^{(i)}_{f'} (L\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S P, M\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S P). \tag{1.8.1} \]
We shall sometimes write \(u\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S P\) instead of \(u\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S\operatorname{Id}_P\).

pages.title: 2. Functoriality of \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}\) and external tensor products.

blocks.html: 2. Functoriality of \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}\) and external tensor products.

2.1 Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a \(S\)-morphism, \(P\in\operatorname{ob}D(Y)\), \(Q\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(Y)\). The obvious canonical arrow at the level of complexes \(f^*\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}^{\boldsymbol\cdot}(P,Q)\longrightarrow \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}^{\boldsymbol\cdot}(f^*P,f^*Q)\) provides by derivation a canonical arrow
\[ f^*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(P,Q)\longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f^*P,f^*Q). \tag{2.1.1} \]
Let \(L\in\operatorname{ob}D(X)\), \(M\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(X)\). Set
\[ \operatorname{Hom}^{(i,j)}_f((L,M),(P,Q)) =\operatorname{Hom}^{(i)}_f(L,P)\times\operatorname{Hom}^{(j)}_f(M,Q), \tag{2.1.2} \]
with the notation 1.4. For \((u,v)\in\operatorname{Hom}^{(2,1)}_f((L,M),(P,Q))\) we define
\[ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(u,v)\in \operatorname{Hom}^{(1)}_f(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(L,M), R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(P,Q)) \tag{2.1.3} \]

by composing 2.1.1 with the arrow \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f^*P,f^*Q)\longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(L,M)\) defined by \(u\) and \(v\). For \(L=f^*P\), \(Q=f_*M\), \(u\) (resp. \(v\)) given by the identity of \(L\) (resp. \(Q\)), \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(u,v)\) is the isomorphism of « trivial duality »
\[ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(P,f_*M)\xrightarrow{\sim} f_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f^*P,M). \tag{2.1.4} \]
For \((u,v)\in\operatorname{Hom}^{(3,4)}_f((L,M),(P,Q))\), we define
\[ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(u,v)\in \operatorname{Hom}^{(1)}_f(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(L,M), R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(P,Q)) \tag{2.1.5} \]
as the composite of the arrows

\[ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(P,Q)\xrightarrow{a} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_!L,Q)\xrightarrow{b} f_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(L,f^!Q)\xrightarrow{c} f_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(L,M), \]
where \(a\) (resp. \(c\)) is defined by \(u\) (resp. \(v\)), and \(b\) is the inverse of the duality isomorphism (SGA 4 XVIII 3.1.9.6).

It follows easily from the definitions that the formation of \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(u,v)\) is compatible with composition: for \((u,v)\), \((u',v')\) composable and of type (2,1) (resp. (3,4)), we have
\[ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(u'u,v'v)= R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(u',v')\, R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(u,v). \tag{2.1.6} \]

2.2 Let \(X\) be an \(S\)-scheme. Recall that for \(L\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(X)\), \(M\in\operatorname{ob}D(X)\), \(N\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(X)\), we have the adjunction isomorphism (« dear to Cartan », as Grothendieck says)
\[ \operatorname{Hom}(L\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}M,N)= \operatorname{Hom}(L,R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(M,N)). \tag{2.2.1} \]
For \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(X)\), \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(X)\), the identity arrow of \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F)\) defines via 2.2.1 an arrow
\[ E\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F) \longrightarrow F, \tag{2.2.2} \]
called the evaluation arrow: it is derived from the arrow at the level of complexes
\[ \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}^{\boldsymbol\cdot}(E,F)\otimes E \longrightarrow F,\qquad f\otimes x\longmapsto f(x). \]
Let, for \(i=1,2\), \(E_i,F_i\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(X)\). By adjunction (2.2.1), the arrow

\[ E_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}E_2 \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E_1,F_1) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E_2,F_2) \longrightarrow F_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}F_2, \]
composed of a symmetry isomorphism and of the tensor product of the evaluation arrows relative to \((E_1,F_1)\) and \((E_2,F_2)\), provides an arrow

\[ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E_1,F_1)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E_2,F_2)\longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}E_2, F_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}F_2). \tag{2.2.3} \]

Let, for \(i=1,2\), \(X_i\) be an \(S\)-scheme, \(X=X_1\times_SX_2\), \(p_i:X\longrightarrow X_i\) the canonical projection, \(E_i,F_i\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(X_i)\), \(E=E_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S E_2\), \(F=F_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S F_2\). We have a canonical arrow
\[ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E_1,F_1) \mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E_2,F_2) \longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F), \tag{2.2.4} \]

composed of the tensor product of the arrows \(p_i^*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E_i,F_i)\longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(p_i^*E_i,p_i^*F_i)\) and of 2.2.3 for \((p_i^*E_i,p_i^*F_i)\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.3 — The arrow 2.2.4 is an isomorphism.

By localization and dévissage, we reduce to \(E_i=f_{i!}A_{U_i}\) for \(f_i:U_i\longrightarrow X_i\) étale. We have canonical isomorphisms (duality)

\[ \begin{gathered} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_{i!}A_{U_i},F_i)\xrightarrow{\sim} f_{i*}f_i^*F_i,\\ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F)\xrightarrow{\sim}f_*f^*F \quad(f=f_1\times_S f_2). \end{gathered} \tag{1} \]
Taking into account the Kunneth formula 1.6.4, it suffices, in order to conclude, to verify that these isomorphisms render commutative the square

\[ \begin{array}{ccc} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_{1!}A_{U_1},F_1) \mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_{2!}A_{U_2},F_2) &\xrightarrow{2.2.3}&R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_!A_U,F)\\ \downarrow\wr&&\downarrow\wr\\ f_{1*}f_1^*F_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S f_{2*}f_2^*F_2 &\xrightarrow{1.6.4}&f_*f^*F, \end{array}\tag{2} \]
where \(U=U_1\times_S U_2\). Now, since \(f_i\) is étale, one sees at once that the isomorphism (1) relative to \(f_i\) (resp. \(f\)) is adjoint to the composite arrow
\[ f_i^*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_{i!}A_{U_i},F_i) \xrightarrow{\sim}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_i^*f_{i!}A_{U_i},f_i^*F_i) \longrightarrow f_i^*F_i, \tag{3} \]

where the second arrow is deduced from the adjunction \(A_{U_i}\longrightarrow f_i^*f_{i!}A_{U_i}\) (resp. the analogous composite arrow with \(f\)). The commutativity of (2) is equivalent to that of the square

\[ \begin{array}{ccc} f_1^*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_{1!}A_{U_1},F_1) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S f_2^*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_{2!}A_{U_2},F_2) &\longrightarrow&f^*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_!A_U,F)\\ \downarrow&&\downarrow\\ f_1^*F_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}f_2^*F_2 &\xrightarrow{\sim}&f^*F, \end{array}\tag{4} \]
where the vertical arrows are given by (3). Now the commutativity of (4) follows from the fact that 2.2.3 is functorial in \(E_i\) and reduces to the identity for \(E_i=A\), whence the proposition.

2.4 Let, for \(i=1,2\), \(f_i:X_i\longrightarrow Y_i\) be an \(S\)-morphism, \(f=f_1\times_Sf_2:X\longrightarrow Y\), \(E_i,F_i\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(X_i)\), \(P_i,Q_i\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(Y_i)\),

\[ E=E_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S E_2,\quad F=F_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S F_2,\quad P=P_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S P_2,\quad Q=Q_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q_2 : \] \[ \begin{array}{ccccc} X_1&\longleftarrow&X_1\times_S Y_2&\longleftarrow&X\\ \downarrow f_1&&\downarrow&&\downarrow\\ Y_1&\longleftarrow&Y&\longleftarrow&Y_1\times_S X_2\\ \downarrow&&\downarrow&&\downarrow\\ S&\longleftarrow&Y_2&\xleftarrow{f_2}&X_2. \end{array}\tag{2.4.0} \]

Let us be given \((u_1,v_1)\in\operatorname{Hom}^{(3,4)}_{f_1}((E_1,F_1),(P_1,Q_1))\), \((u_2,v_2)\in\operatorname{Hom}^{(2,1)}_{f_2}((E_2,F_2),(P_2,Q_2))\) (notation of 2.1.2 and 1.4). By 1.8, we deduce from \((u_i,v_i)\) a square

\[ \begin{array}{ccc} (E_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S P_2, F_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q_2) &\longleftarrow&(E,F)\\ \downarrow{(u_1\boxtimes P_2,v_1\boxtimes Q_2)}&& \downarrow{(u_1\boxtimes E_2,v_1\boxtimes F_2)}\\ (P,Q)&\longleftarrow&(P_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S E_2, Q_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S F_2), \end{array}\tag{2.4.1} \]
where the vertical (resp. horizontal) arrows are of type (3,4) (resp. (2,1)).
We can now state the main compatibility of this section, which will play an essential role in the verification of the Lefschetz-Verdier formula.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.5 — The diagram 2.5.1 below, in which the right-hand square is deduced from 2.4.1 by applying \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}\) in the sense of 2.1.3 and 2.1.5, is commutative:
\[ \begin{array}{ccc} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E_1,F_1)\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E_2,F_2)&\xrightarrow{2.2.4}&R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F)\\ \downarrow{R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(u_1,v_1)\boxtimes R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(u_2,v_2)}&&\downarrow\\ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(P_1,Q_1)\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(P_2,Q_2)&\xrightarrow{2.2.4}&R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(P,Q). \end{array}\tag{2.5.1} \]

2.6 The proof of 2.5 will occupy the rest of nº2. It follows from 1.6.3 that, if \(w_i:L_i\longrightarrow M_i\) is an arrow of type 1 above \(f_i\), the arrow \(w=w_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S w_2\) renders commutative the diagram

\[ \xymatrix{ &L_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S L_2\ar[dl]_{L_1\boxtimes w_2} \ar[dr]^{w_1\boxtimes L_2}&\\ L_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S M_2\ar[dr]_{w_1\boxtimes M_2}&& M_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S L_2\ar[dl]^{M_1\boxtimes w_2}\\ &M_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S M_2.&} \]
Applying this remark to \(w_i=R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(u_i,v_i)\), we see that it suffices to prove the following special cases of 2.5:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.6.1 — Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be an \(S\)-morphism, \(S'\) an \(S\)-scheme, \(f':X'\longrightarrow Y'\) the morphism deduced from \(f\) by the base change \(S'\longrightarrow S\), \(E,F\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(X)\), \(P,Q\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(Y)\), \(P',Q'\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(S')\), \((u,v)\in\operatorname{Hom}^{(2,1)}_f((E,F),(P,Q))\) (resp. \(\operatorname{Hom}^{(3,4)}_f((E,F),(P,Q))\)). We then have a commutative square, in which the vertical arrows are of type 1 above \(f'\):
\[ \begin{array}{ccc} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F)\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(P',Q')&\xrightarrow{2.2.4}& R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S P', F\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q')\\ \downarrow{R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(u,v)\boxtimes R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(P',Q')}&& \downarrow{R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(u\boxtimes P',v\boxtimes Q')}\\ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(P,Q)\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(P',Q')&\xrightarrow{2.2.4}& R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(P\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S P', Q\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q'). \end{array}\tag{2.6.1.1} \]

Let us first examine the case where \((u,v)\) is of type (2,1). By functoriality, we may assume that \(E=f^*P\), \(F=f^*Q\), with \((u,v)\) being given by the identity of \((f^*P,f^*Q)\). The commutativity of 2.6.1.1 in the case \(P'=Q'=A_{S'}\) is obvious; to establish it in the general case, it therefore suffices to verify that for \(L,M\in\operatorname{ob} D_{ctf}(Y)\), the square

\[ \begin{array}{ccc} f^*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes} f^*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(L,M)&\xrightarrow{f^*(2.2.3)}& f^*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}L, F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}M)\\ \downarrow{2.1.1\otimes2.1.1}&&\downarrow{2.1.1}\\ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f^*E,f^*F)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f^*L,f^*M)&\xrightarrow{2.2.3}& R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f^*(E\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}L), f^*(F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}M)) \end{array} \]
is commutative. Now one sees at once that the two composites of this square are adjoint to the tensor product of the evaluation arrows

\[ f^*E\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}f^*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F) \longrightarrow f^*F\quad\text{and}\quad f^*L\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}f^*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(L,M) \longrightarrow f^*M, \]
whence the assertion.

Let us now pass to the case where \((u,v)\) is of type (3,4). By functoriality, we may assume that \(P=f_!E\), \(F=f^!Q\), with \((u,v)\) being defined by \((\operatorname{Id}_P,\operatorname{Id}_F)\). We must prove the commutativity of the square

\[ \begin{array}{ccc} f_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,f^!Q)\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(P',Q')&\xrightarrow{b}& f'_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S P', f^!Q\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q')\\ \downarrow{a\boxtimes R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(P',Q')}&&\downarrow{a'}\\ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_!E,Q)\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(P',Q')&\xrightarrow{2.2.4}& R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_!E\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S P', Q\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q'), \end{array} \]

where \(b\) is the composite of \(f'_*(2.2.4)\) and of a base-change isomorphism, \(a\) is the duality isomorphism (SGA 4 XVIII 3.1.9.6), and \(a'\) the composite of the duality isomorphism (relative to \(E\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S P'\) and \(Q\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q'\)) and of the base-change isomorphisms

\[ f_!E\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S P'\xrightarrow[c]{\sim} f'_!(E\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S P'),\qquad f^!Q\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q'\xrightarrow[d]{\sim} f'^!(Q\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q'). \]
Recall that \(a\) is the composite of the canonical arrow (SGA 4 XVIII 3.1.9.5)
\[ f_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,f^!Q)\longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_!E,f_!f^!Q) \]
and of the arrow defined by the adjunction arrow \(f_!f^!Q\longrightarrow Q\). We have an analogous description of \(a'\), as the composite of the arrow just cited

\[ f'_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S P', f^!Q\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q')\longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f'_!(E\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S P'), f'_!(f^!Q\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q')), \tag{*} \]

of the arrows defined by \(c\) and \(d\), and of the arrow defined by the adjunction arrow \(f'_!f'^!(Q\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q')\longrightarrow Q\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q'\). Now, by definition, \(d\) is adjoint to the arrow \(f'_!(f^!Q\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q')\longrightarrow Q\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q'\) defined by the base-change isomorphism \(f'_!(f^!Q\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q')\simeq f_!f^!Q\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q'\) and the adjunction arrow \(f_!f^!Q\longrightarrow Q\), in other words the square

\[ \begin{array}{ccc} f'_!(f^!Q\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q')&\xrightarrow{f'_!d}& f'_!f'^!(Q\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q')\\ \downarrow{\text{base ch. }\sim}&&\downarrow{\mathrm{adj}}\\ f_!f^!Q\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q'&\xrightarrow{\mathrm{adj}\boxtimes1}& Q\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q' \end{array} \]

is commutative. Consequently, one can also describe \(a'\) as the composite of (*), of the arrow defined by \(c\), and of those defined by the base-change isomorphism \(f'_!(f^!Q\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q')\xrightarrow{\sim} f_!f^!Q\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q'\) and the adjunction arrow \(f_!f^!Q\longrightarrow Q\). This decomposition of \(a'\) and that of \(a\) given above, together with the commutativity of the square

\[ \begin{array}{ccc} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_!E,f_!f^!Q)\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(P',Q')&\longrightarrow& R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_!E\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S P', f_!f^!Q\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q')\\ \downarrow&&\downarrow\\ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_!E,Q)\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(P',Q')&\longrightarrow& R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_!E\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S P', Q\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q') \end{array} \]
defined by 2.2.4 and the adjunction arrow \(f_!f^!Q\longrightarrow Q\), show that, in order to establish the commutativity of 2.6.2, it suffices to prove the following compatibility:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.6.3 — Let \(f\), \(f'\), \(E\), \(F\), \(P'\), \(Q'\) be as in 2.6.1. We then have a commutative square
\[ \begin{array}{ccc} f_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F)\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(P',Q')&\xrightarrow{(1)}& f'_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S P', F\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q')\\ \downarrow{(2)}&&\downarrow{(4)}\\ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_!E,f_!F)\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(P',Q')&\xrightarrow{(3)}& R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_!E\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S P', f_!F\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q'), \end{array} \]

where (1) is the composite of the base-change isomorphism \(f_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F)\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S- \xrightarrow{\sim}f'_*(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F) \mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S-)\) and of \(f'_*(2.2.4)\), (2) is deduced from the canonical arrow \(f_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F)\longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_!E,f_!F)\) (SGA 4 XVIII 3.1.9.5) by applying \(\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}} (P',Q')\), (3) is given by 2.2.4, and finally (4) is the composite of the canonical arrow

\[ f'_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S P', F\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q')\longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f'_!(E\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S P'), f'_!(F\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q')) \]

(loc. cit.) and of the arrows defined by the base-change isomorphisms

\[ f'_!(E\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S P')\xrightarrow{\sim} f_!E\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S P',\qquad f'_!(F\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q')\xrightarrow{\sim} f_!F\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q'. \]

Going back to the definition of the arrow (SGA 4 XVIII 3.1.9.5), one chooses a compactification of \(f\)(1), and we are reduced to proving the lemma separately in the case where \(f\) is an open immersion and that where \(f\) is a proper morphism.

a) Case where \(f\) is an open immersion. By adjunction (2.2.1), it suffices to see that if one tensors the square of 2.6.3 by \(f_!E\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S P'\) and composes with the evaluation arrow 2.2.2

\[ (f_!E\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S P')\mathbin{\overset L\otimes} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_!E\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S P', f_!F\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q')\longrightarrow f_!F\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q', \]
the square obtained is commutative. Now the vertices of this square vanish outside \(X'\), so that it suffices to verify that the square induced on \(X'\) commutes, which is trivial.

b) Case where \(f\) is a proper morphism. We then have \(f_!=f_*\), \(f'_!=f'_*\). One sees easily that the composite of \(f_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F)\longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_*E,f_*F)\) with the trivial duality isomorphism
\[ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_*E,f_*F)\xrightarrow{\sim} f_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f^*f_*E,F) \tag{2.1.4} \]

is none other than the arrow deduced from the adjunction arrow \(f^*f_*E\longrightarrow E\) by applying \(f_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(-,F)\). Similarly, the composite of (4) with the trivial duality isomorphism

\[ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_*E,f_*F)\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(P',Q')\xrightarrow{\sim} f'_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f^*f_*E\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S P', F\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q') \]

(\(f_*F\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q'\) being identified with \(f'_*(F\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q')\) by the canonical isomorphism) is none other than the arrow deduced from the adjunction arrow \(f^*f_*E\longrightarrow E\) by applying \(f'_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(-\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S P', F\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q')\). We are therefore reduced to verifying the commutativity of the square

\[ \xymatrix{ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_*E,f_*F)\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(P',Q')\ar[r]^{(3)}\ar[d]& R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_*E\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S P', f_*F\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q')\ar[d]\\ f_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f^*f_*E,F)\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(P',Q')\ar[r]^{(1')}& f'_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f^*f_*E\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S P', F\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S Q'), } \]

where the vertical arrows are the trivial duality isomorphisms and \((1')\) is (1) with \(E\) replaced by \(f^*f_*E\). But the latter is a special case of that of 2.6.1.1 in the case, already treated, where \((u,v)\) is of type (2,1) (take \(u:f^*f_*E\longrightarrow f_*E\) given by the identity of \(f^*f_*E\), and \(v:F\longrightarrow f_*F\) given by the identity of \(f_*F\)). We have thus proved 2.6.3. As we have seen, the commutativity of 2.6.2 follows from it, which completes the proof of 2.6.1 and, finally, of 2.5.

footnotes.html:
The arrow cited is defined
by means of such a compactification, but does not depend on it: this point,
tacitly assumed in (loc. cit.), is easily verified.

blocks.html: 3. Cohomological correspondences.

3.1 Let, for \(i=1,2\), \(X_i\) be an \(S\)-scheme, \(L_i\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(X_i)\), \(X=X_1\times_S X_2\), \(p_i:X\longrightarrow X_i\) and \(q_i:X_i\longrightarrow S\) the canonical projections:
\[ \xymatrix{ &X\ar[dl]_{p_1}\ar[dr]^{p_2}&\\ L_1\quad X_1\ar[dr]_{q_1}&&X_2\quad L_2\ar[dl]^{q_2}\\ &S.&} \]
With the notation of 1.3, we have a canonical isomorphism (2.2.4)
\[ DL_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S L_2\xrightarrow{\sim} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(L_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S A_{X_2}, q_1^!A_S\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S L_2). \]
Composing with the Kunneth isomorphism \(q_1^!A_S\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S L_2\xrightarrow{\sim}p_2^!L_2\) (1.7.3), we obtain an isomorphism
\[ DL_1\mathbin{\overset L\boxtimes}_S L_2\xrightarrow{\sim} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(p_1^*L_1,p_2^!L_2). \tag{3.1.1} \]

The latter will play a fundamental role in all that follows. For \(X_1=S\), 3.1.1 reduces to \(L_1^\vee\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}L_2\xrightarrow{\sim} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(L_1,L_2)\), a special case of an isomorphism valid for perfect complexes on an arbitrary ringed topos (SGA 6 I 7.7). We shall set
\[ \begin{aligned} R\operatorname{Hom}_S(L_1,L_2)&=R\operatorname{Hom}(p_1^*L_1,p_2^!L_2),\\ \operatorname{Hom}_S(L_1,L_2)&=\operatorname{Hom}(p_1^*L_1,p_2^!L_2). \end{aligned} \tag{3.1.2} \]

The elements of \(\operatorname{Hom}_S(L_1,L_2)\) will be called cohomological correspondences from \(L_1\) to \(L_2\). Instead of "cohomological correspondence from \(A_{X_1}\) to \(A_{X_2}\)" we shall say simply "cohomological correspondence from \(X_1\) to \(X_2\)" (understood: with coefficients in \(A\)).
If \(X_1\) is smooth, purely of relative dimension \(r_1\), the same is true of \(p_2\), and by the duality theorem (SGA 4 XVIII 3.2.5), we have
hence

\[ \begin{aligned} R\operatorname{Hom}_S(L_1,L_2)&= R\operatorname{Hom}(p_1^*L_1,p_2^*L_2)(r_1)[2r_1],\\ \operatorname{Hom}_S(L_1,L_2)&= H^{2r_1}(X,R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(p_1^*L_1,p_2^*L_2)(r_1)). \end{aligned} \tag{3.1.3} \]
In particular, the group of cohomological correspondences from \(X_1\) to \(X_2\) is identified in this case with \(H^{2r_1}(X,A(r_1))\).

3.2 The cohomological correspondences one meets in practice are associated with \(S\)-morphisms \(X_2\longrightarrow X_1\), or more generally with cycles of \(X\), or with schemes over \(X\). Let \(c:C\longrightarrow X\) be a scheme over \(X\) (one sometimes says that \(c\) is a correspondence between \(X_1\) and \(X_2\)), and denote by \(c_i=p_i c:C\longrightarrow X_i\) the projections. We shall call cohomological correspondences from \(L_1\) to \(L_2\) with support in \(c\) (or \(C\), if there is no risk of confusion) the elements of the group \(H^0(X,c_!R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(c_1^*L_1,c_2^!L_2))\) (\(=\operatorname{Hom}(c_1^*L_1,c_2^!L_2)\) if \(c\) is proper). By the induction formula (SGA 4 XVIII 3.1.12.2), we have a canonical isomorphism
\[ c^!R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(p_1^*L_1,p_2^!L_2) \xrightarrow{\sim}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(c_1^*L_1,c_2^!L_2), \tag{3.2.1} \]
whence, by applying \(c_!\) and composing with the adjunction arrow \(c_!c^!\longrightarrow\operatorname{Id}\), canonical arrows

\[ c_!R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(c_1^*L_1,c_2^!L_2) \longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(p_1^*L_1,p_2^!L_2), \tag{3.2.2} \] \[ H^0(X,c_!R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(c_1^*L_1,c_2^!L_2)) \longrightarrow\operatorname{Hom}_S(L_1,L_2). \tag{3.2.3} \]
Every correspondence from \(L_1\) to \(L_2\) with support in \(C\) therefore defines canonically, by 3.2.3, a correspondence (without support) from \(L_1\) to \(L_2\).
Examples: a) Suppose \(c\) proper and \(c_2\) of finite tor-dimension and of relative dimension \(\leq N\). The trace morphism (SGA 41/2 Cycle 2.3.3)
\[ \operatorname{Tr}_{c_2}:c_{2!}A_C\longrightarrow A_{X_2}(-N)[-2N] \]
defines by adjunction a cohomological correspondence from \(A_{X_1}\) to \(A_{X_2}(-N)[-2N]\)
with support in \(C\), which will be called the class of \((c,N)\), and denoted

The most important case is that where \(N=0\), i.e. \(c_2\) finite (and of finite tor-dimension), in which case \(cl(c)\) is a cohomological correspondence from \(X_1\) to \(X_2\) with support in \(C\). If \(X_1\) is smooth, purely of relative dimension \(r_1\), we have by 3.1.3 and 3.2.1,

If moreover \(c\) is a closed immersion, the right-hand side can be rewritten \(H_C^{2(r_1-N)}(X,A_X(r_1-N))\), and the class of \((c,N)\), considered as an element of this group, is none other than the cohomology class associated with \(c\) in the sense of (SGA 41/2 Cycle 2.3.1), as follows at once from the definitions.

b) Let \(F:X_2\longrightarrow X_1\) be an \(S\)-morphism, and denote by \(c(F):C(F)\longrightarrow X\) its graph, i.e. the image of the section of \(p_2\) defined by \(F\) ("set of points \((Fx,x)\)"): \(c_2\) is an isomorphism, and \(c_1c_2^{-1}=F\). We therefore have canonical isomorphisms

\[ \begin{aligned} R\operatorname{Hom}(c_1(F)^*L_1,c_2(F)^!L_2)&= R\operatorname{Hom}(F^*L_1,L_2),\\ \operatorname{Hom}(c_1(F)^*L_1,c_2(F)^!L_2)&= \operatorname{Hom}(F^*L_1,L_2). \end{aligned} \tag{3.2.6} \]

In other words, the cohomological correspondences from \(L_1\) to \(L_2\) with support in the graph of \(F\) are the "liftings" of \(F\) to a homomorphism \(F^*L_1\longrightarrow L_2\). Here are two important examples of such correspondences:

(i) \(X_1=X_2\), \(L_1=L_2\), \(F=\) the identity of \(X_1\), whose graph \(\Delta\) is the diagonal. The correspondence with support in \(\Delta\) defined by the identity of \(L_1\) is called the diagonal correspondence. It is the element
\[ 1\in\operatorname{Hom}(L_1,L_1)= H^0_\Delta(X,R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(L_1,L_1)) \]
(a group which is identified with
\[ H^{2r}_\Delta(X,R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(L_1,L_1)(r)) \quad\text{when }X_1\text{ is smooth, purely of relative dimension }r). \]

(ii) We suppose that \(S\) is the spectrum of a finite field \(\mathbb{F}_q\), that \(X_1=X_2\), \(L_1=L_2\), and we take for \(F:X_1\longrightarrow X_1\) the Frobenius endomorphism relative to \(\mathbb{F}_q\), the identity on the underlying space and raising to the \(q\)-th power on \(\mathcal{O}_{X_1}\) (cf. XIV), and as lifting of \(F\) to \(L_1\) the canonical isomorphism \(F^*L_1\xrightarrow{\sim}L_1\) ((loc. cit. §2 prop. 1) (more exactly, the \(a\)-th iterate of the isomorphism of (loc. cit.), if \(q=p^a\)). The cohomological correspondence from \(L_1\) to \(L_1\) with support in the graph of \(F\) thus defined is called the Frobenius correspondence (relative to \(\mathbb{F}_q\)).
c) Certain cohomological correspondences between sheaves on curves, related to the Hecke operators, have been studied by Langlands in ([13] §7).

d) Cohomological correspondences between constant sheaves (the most frequent in nature) provide in a natural way, by "integration", cohomological correspondences between complexes, as we shall now see.

3.3 Let, for \(i=1,2\), \(f_i:X_i\longrightarrow Y_i\) be an \(S\)-morphism, \(f=f_1\times_S f_2:X\longrightarrow Y\), \(f'_1=f_1\times_S Y_2\), \(f'_2=Y_1\times_S f_2\), \(p'_1=X_1\times_S f_2\), \(p'_2=f_1\times_S X_2\):
\[ \xymatrix{ X_1\times_S Y_2\ar[d]_{f'_1}&X\ar[l]_{p'_1}\ar[dl]^f\ar[d]^{p'_2}\\ Y&Y_1\times_S X_2\ar[l]^{f'_2} }. \tag{3.3.0} \]
Let \(L_i\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(X_i)\). There exists a unique isomorphism
\[ f_*R\operatorname{Hom}_S(L_1,L_2)\xrightarrow{\sim} R\operatorname{Hom}_S(f_{1!}L_1,f_{2*}L_2) \tag{3.3.1} \]
whose inverse renders commutative the square

\[ \begin{CD} D(f_{1!}L_1)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S f_{2*}L_2 @>{3.1.1}>> R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(f_{1!}L_1,f_{2*}L_2)\\ @V{(1)}VV @VVV\\ f_{1*}D L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S f_{2*}L_2 @>{(2)}>> f_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(L_1,L_2), \end{CD} \]

where (1) is deduced from the inverse of the duality isomorphism (SGA 4 XVIII 3.1.9.6) \(D f_{1!}L_1\xrightarrow{\sim}f_{1*}D L_1\) by applying \(\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S f_{2*}L_2\), and (2) is the composite of the Künneth isomorphism \(f_{1*}D L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S f_{2*}L_2 \xrightarrow{\sim}f_*(D L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_2)\) and of \(f_*(3.1.1)\). By applying \(R\Gamma(Y,-)\) one deduces from 3.3.1 an isomorphism
\[ f_*:\operatorname{Hom}_S(L_1,L_2)\xrightarrow{\sim} \operatorname{Hom}_S(f_{1!}L_1,f_{2*}L_2). \tag{3.3.2} \]

For \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}_S(L_1,L_2)\), the correspondence \(f_*u\) will be called the direct image of \(u\) by \(f\). When \(Y_1=Y_2=S\), we shall write \(u_*\) rather than \(f_*u\). If \(Y_1=Y_2=S\) is the spectrum of a separably closed field, 3.3.2 reads
\[ \operatorname{Hom}_S(L_1,L_2)\xrightarrow{\sim} \operatorname{Hom}(R\Gamma_c(X_1,L_1),R\Gamma(X_2,L_2)). \tag{3.3.3} \]

Thus, cohomological correspondences "act" on cohomology. The statement below will allow us to interpret this action in various ways, and in particular to make the link with the usual definitions in situations such as a) or b) above.
Proposition 3.4 — With the notation of 3.3.0, we have a commutative diagram of isomorphisms of \(D_{ctf}(Y)\):

\[ \xymatrix{ &R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(f_{1!}L_1,f_{2*}L_2) \ar[dl]_{(1)}\ar[dr]^{(2)}\ar[d]&\\ f'_{1*}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(L_1,f_{2*}L_2) \ar[dr]_{f'_{1*}(3)}&& f'_{2*}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(f_{1!}L_1,L_2) \ar[dl]^{f'_{2*}(4)}\\ &f_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(L_1,L_2)& } \tag{3.4.1} \]
where (5) is the inverse of 3.3.1 and the arrows (1) to (4) are defined by the commutative diagrams below, in which "ch. b." denotes an arrow deduced

from a base-change or Künneth isomorphism of one of the types considered in nº1, "dual." an arrow deduced from a duality isomorphism of type 2.1.4 or (SGA 4 XVIII 3.1.9.6), "triv." a trivial isomorphism.

\[ \begin{CD} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_{1!}L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S A_{Y_2}, K_{Y_1}\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S f_{2*}L_2) @>{\mathrm{ch.b.}}>> R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(f_{1!}L_1,f_{2*}L_2)\\ @V{\mathrm{ch.b.}}VV @VV{(1)}V\\ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f'_{1!}(L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S A_{Y_2}), K_{Y_1}\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S f_{2*}L_2) @.\\ @V{\mathrm{dual.}}VV @.\\ f'_{1*}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S A_{Y_2}, f_1'^!(K_{Y_1}\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S f_{2*}L_2)) @.\\ @V{\mathrm{ch.b.}}VV @.\\ f'_{1*}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S A_{Y_2}, K_{X_1}\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S f_{2*}L_2) @>{\mathrm{ch.b.}}>> f'_{1*}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(L_1,f_{2*}L_2) \end{CD} \] \[ \begin{CD} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_{1!}L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S A_{Y_2}, K_{Y_1}\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S f_{2*}L_2) @>{\mathrm{ch.b.}}>> R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(f_{1!}L_1,f_{2*}L_2)\\ @V{\mathrm{ch.b.}}VV @VV{(2)}V\\ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_{1!}L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S A_{Y_2}, f_2'^!(K_{Y_1}\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_2)) @.\\ @V{\mathrm{dual.}}VV @.\\ f'_{2*}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_2'^*(f_{1!}L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S A_{Y_2}), K_{Y_1}\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_2) @.\\ @V{\mathrm{triv.}}VV @.\\ f'_{2*}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_{1!}L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S A_{X_2}, K_{Y_1}\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_2) @>{\mathrm{ch.b.}}>> f'_{2*}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(f_{1!}L_1,L_2) \end{CD} \]

\[ \begin{CD} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S A_{Y_2}, K_{X_1}\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S f_{2*}L_2) @>{\mathrm{ch.b.}}>> R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(L_1,f_{2*}L_2)\\ @V{\mathrm{ch.b.}}VV @VV{(3)}V\\ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S A_{Y_2}, p_1'^!(K_{X_1}\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_2)) @.\\ @V{\mathrm{dual.}}VV @.\\ p'_{1*}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(p_1'^*(L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S A_{Y_2}), K_{X_1}\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_2) @.\\ @V{\mathrm{triv.}}VV @.\\ p'_{1*}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S A_{X_2}, K_{X_1}\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_2) @>{\mathrm{ch.b.}}>> p'_{1*}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(L_1,L_2) \end{CD} \] \[ \begin{CD} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_{1!}L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S A_{X_2}, K_{Y_1}\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_2) @>{\mathrm{ch.b.}}>> R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(f_{1!}L_1,L_2)\\ @V{\mathrm{ch.b.}}VV @VV{(4)}V\\ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(p'_{2!}(L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S A_{X_2}), K_{Y_1}\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_2) @.\\ @V{\mathrm{dual.}}VV @.\\ p'_{2*}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S A_{X_2}, p_2'^!(K_{Y_1}\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_2)) @.\\ @V{\mathrm{ch.b.}}VV @.\\ p'_{2*}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S A_{X_2}, K_{X_1}\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_2) @>{\mathrm{ch.b.}}>> p'_{2*}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(L_1,L_2). \end{CD} \]

This is merely an explication of 2.5 in the special case where \((u_1,v_1):(L_1,K_{X_1})\longrightarrow(f_{1!}L_1,K_{Y_1})\) is given by the identity of \(f_{1!}L_1\) and the canonical isomorphism \(f_1^!K_{Y_1}\xrightarrow{\sim}K_{X_1}\), and \((u_2,v_2):(A_{X_2},L_2)\longrightarrow(A_{Y_2},f_{2*}L_2)\) by the canonical isomorphism \(A_{X_2}\xrightarrow{\sim}f_2^*A_{Y_2}\) and the identity of \(f_{2*}L_2\).

Corollary 3.5 — We suppose that \(Y_1=Y_2=S\). Let \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}_S(L_1,L_2)\). The direct image \(f_*u\in\operatorname{Hom}(f_{1!}L_1,f_{2*}L_2)\) (3.3.2) can be obtained in the two following equivalent ways:

a) One identifies \(u\), by trivial duality, with an arrow \(L_1\longrightarrow p_{1*}p_2^!L_2\); one composes with the isomorphism of base change \(p'_{1*}p_2'^!L_2\xrightarrow{\sim}f_1^!f_{2*}L_2\); \(f_*u\) is the adjoint of this composite arrow.

b) One identifies \(u\), by adjunction, with an arrow \(p_{2!}p_1^*L_1\longrightarrow L_2\); one composes with the isomorphism of base change \(f_2^*f_{1!}L_1\xrightarrow{\sim}p_{2!}p_1'^*L_1\); \(f_*u\) is the adjoint of this composite arrow.
Description a) (resp. b)) corresponds in fact to the path \((f_{1*}'(3)\circ(1))^{-1}\) (resp. \(f'_{2*}(4)\circ(2)\)) of 3.4.1.

3.6 In practice, it is above all description 3.5 b) that is used. Let us make it explicit at the level of cohomology classes, assuming, to lighten the notation, that \(S\) is the spectrum of a separably closed field. Let \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}_S(L_1,L_2)= \operatorname{Hom}(p_1^*L_1,p_2^!L_2)\). For \(x\in H_c^i(X_1,L_1)\), \(u_*(x)\in H^i(X_2,L_2)\) is defined as follows: one considers the element \(p_1^*x\in H_c^i(X_2,p_{2!}p_1^*L_1)\) deduced from \(x\) by inverse image, one applies \(u\) to it, i.e. one forms the product \(u.p_1^*x\in H^i(X_2,p_{2!}p_2^!L_2)\), then one applies the arrow \(a:H^i(X_2,p_{2!}p_2^!L_2)\longrightarrow H^i(X_2,L_2)\) defined by the adjunction arrow \(p_{2!}p_2^!\longrightarrow\operatorname{Id}\); in summary:

Let \(c:C\longrightarrow X\) be a proper morphism, and \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}(c_1^*L_1,c_2^!L_2)\). Denote by \(u_*\in\operatorname{Hom}(R\Gamma_c(X_1,L_1),R\Gamma(X_2,L_2))\) the image of \(u\) under the composite of 3.2.3 and 3.3.3. It follows from 3.6.1 that, for \(x\in H_c^i(X_1,L_1)\), we have

where \(c_1^*x\) is the inverse image of \(x\) in \(H_c^i(X_2,c_{2!}c_1^*L_1)\) and \(a:H^i(X_2,c_{2!}c_2^!L_2)\longrightarrow H^i(X_2,L_2)\) is the arrow deduced from the adjunction \(c_{2!}c_2^!L_2\longrightarrow\operatorname{Id}\). In the situation of example 3.2 a), one thus finds that
\[ c\ell(c,N)_*(x)=\operatorname{Tr}_{c_2}(c_1^*x), \tag{3.6.3} \]

where \(\operatorname{Tr}_{c_2}:H^i(X_2,Rc_{2!}A_C)\longrightarrow H^{i-2N}(X_2,A(-N))\) is the arrow induced by the trace morphism relative to \(c_2\). This formula agrees, for \(N=0\), with (SGA 41/2 Cycle 3.6): \(c\ell(c)_*\) is the homomorphism denoted \(\eta_{X_2X_1}\) in (loc. cit.). If \(X_1\) is smooth, purely of relative dimension \(r_1\), and \(c\) a closed immersion, it follows from 3.6.1 and from the remark made at the end of 3.2 a) that we also have
\[ c\ell(c,N)_*(x)=\operatorname{Tr}_{p_2}(c\ell(C)p_1^*x), \tag{3.6.4} \]

where \(c\ell(C)\in H_C^{2(r_1-N)}(X,A(r_1-N))\) is the cohomology class of \(C\) and \(\operatorname{Tr}_{p_2}:H^*(X_2,Rp_{2!}A_X)\longrightarrow H^{*-2r_1}(X_2,A(-r_1))\) the arrow defined by the trace morphism relative to \(p_2\). We recover the usual definition of the homomorphism induced on cohomology by a correspondence ([12] 1.3), (SGA 41/2 Cycle 3.2).

3.6.5. In the situation of 3.2 b), it follows from 3.6.2 that, for \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}(F^*L_1,L_2)\), \(u_*:H_c^i(X_1,L_1)\longrightarrow H^i(X_2,L_2)\) is the composite of the "inverse image" homomorphism \(H_c^i(X_1,L_1)\longrightarrow H^i(X_2,F^*L_1)\) and of the homomorphism induced by \(u\) (more generally \(f_*u:Rf_{1!}L_1\longrightarrow Rf_{2*}L_2\) is the composite of the canonical homomorphism \(Rf_{1!}L_1\longrightarrow Rf_{2*}F^*L_1\) and of \(Rf_{2*}u\)). In particular, the diagonal correspondence induces the canonical arrow \(Rf_{1!}L_1\longrightarrow Rf_{1*}L_1\) (the identity if \(X_1\) is proper over \(S\)).
3.7 Let us return to the situation of the beginning of 3.3, assuming given in addition a commutative square
\[ \xymatrix{ X& C\ar[l]_c\\ Y\ar[u]^f& D\ar[l]^d\ar[u]_g } \tag{3.7.1} \]

with \(c\) proper. Set \(c_i=p_i c\), \(d_i=q_i d\), where \(p_i:X\longrightarrow X_i\), \(q_i:Y\longrightarrow Y_i\) are the projections. From 3.7.1 we deduce a commutative diagram in which the inner square is cartesian:
\[ \xymatrix{ &&C\ar[dll]_c\ar[dl]_i\ar[ddl]^g\\ X\ar[d]_f&C'\ar[l]_{c'}\ar[d]^{g'}\\ Y&D\ar[l]^d } \tag{3.7.2} \]
For \(K\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(X)\), we have a canonical arrow
\[ f_*c_*c'^!K\longrightarrow d_*d'^!f_*K, \tag{3.7.3} \]
composed of the arrow
\[ f_*c_*c'^!K=f_*c_*i_*i^!c'^!K\longrightarrow f_*c_*c'^!K=d_*g_*c'^!K \]
defined by the adjunction arrow \(i_*i^!\longrightarrow\operatorname{Id}\), and of the isomorphism
\[ d_*g_*c'^!K\xrightarrow{\sim}d_*d'^!f_*K \]

defined by the canonical isomorphism \(g_*d'^!\xrightarrow{\sim}d'^!f_*\) (SGA 4 XVIII 3.1.12.3). Taking \(K=R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(L_1,L_2)\), and composing 3.7.3 with the isomorphism deduced from 3.3.1, we find a canonical arrow
\[ f_*c_*c'^!R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(L_1,L_2) \longrightarrow d_*d'^!R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(f_{1!}L_1,f_{2*}L_2), \tag{3.7.4} \]
which, thanks to 3.2.1, can be rewritten
\[ f_*c_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(c_1^*L_1,c_2^!L_2) \longrightarrow d_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(d_1^*(f_{1!}L_1),d_2^!(f_{2*}L_2)). \tag{3.7.5} \]
From this we deduce
\[ f_*:\operatorname{Hom}(c_1^*L_1,c_2^!L_2)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(d_1^*(f_{1!}L_1),d_2^!(f_{2*}L_2)). \tag{3.7.6} \]

The arrow 3.7.5 (resp. 3.7.6) generalizes 3.3.1 (resp. 3.3.2), with which it coincides when \(c\) and \(d\) are the identity. Note also that, for \(c\) proper, 3.2.2 (resp. 3.2.3) is none other than 3.7.5 (resp. 3.7.6) when \(f_1\), \(f_2\) and \(d\) are the identity arrows. When \(Y_1=Y_2=S=D\), we shall abbreviate \(f_*u\) to \(u_*\), as above.
4. Pairings of correspondences. Lefschetz formula.

4.1 Let, for \(i=1,2\), \(X_i\) be a \(S\)-scheme, \(X=X_1\times_SX_2\), \(p_i:X\longrightarrow X_i\) the projections, \(L_i\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(X_i)\), and \(K_{X_i}\) the dualizing complex (1.3). The canonical arrows
\[ p_i^*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(L_i,K_{X_i}) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}p_i^*L_i\longrightarrow p_i^*K_{X_i}, \tag{4.1.1} \]
composed of 2.1.1 and of the evaluation arrow 2.2.2, give, by tensor product over \(X\) and composition with the Künneth isomorphism 1.7.6, a pairing
\[ (DL_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_2)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes} (L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S DL_2)\longrightarrow K_X, \tag{4.1.2} \]
which can be rewritten, thanks to 3.1.1, 3.1.2,
\[ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(L_1,L_2)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(L_2,L_1)\longrightarrow K_X. \tag{4.1.3} \]
From this we deduce a pairing, called the cup-product,
\[ \langle\ ,\ \rangle:\operatorname{Hom}_S(L_1,L_2)\otimes \operatorname{Hom}_S(L_2,L_1)\longrightarrow H^0(X,K_X). \tag{4.1.4} \]

One may also consider 4.1.2 as the external tensor product of the evaluation arrows \(DL_i\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}L_i\longrightarrow K_{X_i}\). Taking into account the Künneth isomorphism 2.2.4, one thus sees that 4.1.2 is a "perfect duality", i.e. identifies via the adjunction isomorphism 2.2.1 each of the complexes \(DL_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_2\), \(L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S DL_2\) with the dual of the other (with values in \(K_X\)).
When \(X_1=X_2=S\), 4.1.3 is a special case of a pairing valid for perfect complexes on an arbitrary ringed topos (SGA 6 I 7.8), and, with the notation of (SGA 6 I 8.3), we have
\[ \langle u,v\rangle=\operatorname{Tr}(vu)=\operatorname{Tr}(uv). \tag{4.1.5} \]

4.2 Let \(c:C\longrightarrow X\), \(d:D\longrightarrow X\), \(e=c\times_Xd:E=C\times_XD\longrightarrow X\). For \(P,Q\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(X)\), we have a canonical arrow (which is not an isomorphism in general)
\[ c'^!P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}d'^!Q\longrightarrow e'^!(P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Q), \tag{4.2.1} \]
defined, in the same way as 1.7.3, as the adjoint of the composite arrow
\[ e_!(c'^!P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_X d'^!Q) \xrightarrow[(1)]{\sim}c_!c'^!P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_X d_!d'^!Q \xrightarrow{(2)}P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Q, \]

where (1) is the Künneth isomorphism (SGA 4 XVII 5.4.2.2) and (2) the tensor product of the adjunction arrows. Applying \(e_!\) to 4.2.1 and composing with the Künneth isomorphism just cited, we obtain a canonical arrow
\[ c_!c'^!P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}d_!d'^!Q \longrightarrow e_!e'^!(P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Q). \tag{4.2.2} \]

Similarly, composing the Künneth arrow \(c_*c'^!P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}d_*d'^!Q\longrightarrow e_*(c'^!P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_X d'^!Q)\) with the arrow deduced from 4.2.1 by applying \(e_*\), we obtain a canonical arrow
\[ c_*c'^!P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}d_*d'^!Q \longrightarrow e_*e'^!(P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Q). \tag{4.2.3} \]

For \(P=DL_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_2\), \(Q=L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S DL_2\), we deduce from 4.2.2, 4.2.3, by composition with 4.1.2 and the isomorphism \(e'^!K_X=K_E\), and with the identifications 3.1.1, 3.2.1, pairings

\[ c_!R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(c_1^*L_1,c_2^!L_2) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes} d_!R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(d_2^*L_2,d_1^!L_1) \longrightarrow e_!K_E, \tag{4.2.4} \] \[ c_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(c_1^*L_1,c_2^!L_2) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes} d_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(d_2^*L_2,d_1^!L_1) \longrightarrow e_*K_E, \tag{4.2.4}' \] \[ \langle\ ,\ \rangle:\operatorname{Hom}(c_1^*L_1,c_2^!L_2)\otimes \operatorname{Hom}(d_2^*L_2,d_1^!L_1)\longrightarrow H^0(E,K_E). \tag{4.2.5} \]
These pairings are compatible with étale localization: given a commutative square (not necessarily cartesian)
\[ \xymatrix{E'\ar[d]&C'\ar[l]\ar[d]&D'\ar[l]\ar[d]\\ E&C\ar[l]&D\ar[l]}\qquad\text{étale over}\qquad X, \]

if we denote by \(c'\), \(d'\), \(e'\) the composites \(C'\longrightarrow C\longrightarrow X\), \(D'\longrightarrow D\longrightarrow X\), \(E'\longrightarrow E\longrightarrow X\), we have a commutative square

\[ \xymatrix{ \operatorname{Hom}(c_1^*L_1,c_2^!L_2)\otimes \operatorname{Hom}(d_2^*L_2,d_1^!L_1)\ar[r]^{4.2.5}\ar[d]& H^0(E,K_E)\ar[d]\\ \operatorname{Hom}(c_1'^*L_1,c_2'^!L_2)\otimes \operatorname{Hom}(d_2'^*L_2,d_1'^!L_1)\ar[r]&H^0(E',K_{E'}) } \tag{4.2.6} \]

where the vertical arrows are the restrictions, and the lower horizontal arrow is composed of the arrow analogous to 4.2.5 and of the restriction \(H^0(E'',K_{E''})\longrightarrow H^0(E',K_{E'})\), with \(E''=C'\times_XD'\) (there are analogous commutative squares with arrows of type 4.2.4, 4.2.4', which we leave to the reader to write down). If \(Z\) is étale over \(E\), we shall denote, for \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}(c_1^*L_1,c_2^!L_2)\), \(v\in\operatorname{Hom}(d_2^*L_2,d_1^!L_1)\),
\[ \langle u,v\rangle_Z\in H^0(Z,K_Z) \tag{4.2.7} \]

the image of \(\langle u,v\rangle\) in \(H^0(Z,K_Z)\) by restriction (in practice, \(Z\) will be a connected component of \(E\)). If \(z\) is an isolated closed point of \(E\), and \(\bar z\) a geometric point above \(z\), it follows from 4.2.6 that \(\langle u,v\rangle_z\in H^0(z,K_z)=A\) depends only on the situation deduced by strict localization at the images of \(\bar z\) in \(C,D,X\).

Example 4.3 — We suppose that \(X_i\) is smooth, purely of relative dimension \(r_i\), and that \(c\) and \(d\) are closed immersions, with \(c_2\) (resp. \(d_1\)) finite. For \(L_1=A_{X_1}\), \(L_2=A_{X_2}\), we have, by 3.1.3,
\[ DL_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_2=A_X(r_1)[2r_1],\qquad L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S DL_2=A_X(r_2)[2r_2], \]
and 4.1.2 is identified with the product
\[ A_X(r_1)[2r_1]\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}A_X(r_2)[2r_2] \longrightarrow A_X(r)[2r] \]
(where \(r=r_1+r_2\)), and consequently, taking 3.2.1 into account, 4.2.5 is identified with the usual cup-product
\[ H_C^{2r_1}(X,A(r_1))\otimes H_D^{2r_2}(X,A(r_2)) \longrightarrow H_{C\cap D}^{2r}(X,A(r)). \]

Let us take as cohomological correspondence from \(X_1\) to \(X_2\) (resp. \(X_2\) to \(X_1\)) the class of \(c\) (resp. \(d\)) (3.2.a)). It follows from (SGA 41/2 Cycle 2.3.8) that at an isolated point
\(z\) of \(C\cap D\), \(\langle cl(c),cl(d)\rangle_z\) is none other than the image in \(A\) of the intersection multiplicity of \(C\) and \(D\) at \(z\).
Here now is the main result of the exposé.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.4.

Let, for \(i=1,2\), \(f_i:X_i\longrightarrow Y_i\) be a proper \(S\)-morphism, \(f=f_1\times_Sf_2:X\longrightarrow Y\), \(p_i:X\longrightarrow X_i\), \(q_i:Y\longrightarrow Y_i\) the projections. Let moreover

\[ \xymatrix{ &C'\ar[dl]_{c'}&C\ar[l]\ar[dl]_c\ar[d]^g\\ X&C''\ar[l]_{c''}\ar[u]\ar[d]^{f'}&D\ar[l]\ar[d]\\ Y&D''\ar[l]_{d''}&D'\ar[l]\ar[ul]_{d'} } \tag{4.4.0} \]

be a commutative cube with cartesian horizontal faces, with \(c'\), \(c''\), \(d'\), \(d''\) proper. We set \(c_i'=p_ic'\), \(c_i''=p_ic''\), \(d_i'=q_id'\), \(d_i''=q_id''\). Finally, let \(L_i\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(X_i)\). We then have a commutative square

\[ \xymatrix{ f_*c_*'R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(c_1'^*L_1,c_2'^!L_2) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes} f_*c_*''R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(c_2''^*L_2,c_1''^!L_1) \ar[r]^{(1)}\ar[d]_{(2)}&f_*c_*K_C\ar[d]^{(4)}\\ d_*'R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(d_1'^*M_1,d_2'^!M_2) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes} d_*''R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(d_2''^*M_2,d_1''^!M_1) \ar[r]^{(3)}&d_*K_D } \tag{4.4.1} \]

where \(M_i=f_{i*}L_i\), (3) is given by 4.2.4, (1) is deduced from 4.2.4 by applying \(f_*\) and composing with the canonical arrow \(f_*c_*'E\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}f_*c_*''F\longrightarrow f_*(c_*'E\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}c_*''F)\), (2) is the tensor product of the arrows 3.7.5 relative to the faces containing \(f\), and (4) is defined by the adjunction arrow \(g_*K_C=g_*g^!K_D\longrightarrow K_D\).

Proof — Set \(DL_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_2=P\), \(L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S DL_2=Q\), \(DM_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S M_2=E\), \(M_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S DM_2=F\). Consider the diagram

\[ \xymatrix{ f_*c_*'c'^!P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}f_*c_*''c''^!Q\ar[r]^{(1)}\ar[d]_{(8)}& f_*c_*c^!(P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Q)\ar[r]^{(2)}\ar[d]_{(9)}&f_*c_*c^!K_X\ar[d]^{(10)}\\ d_*'d'^!f_*P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}d_*''d''^!f_*Q\ar[r]^{(3)}\ar[d]_{(11)}& d_*d'^!(f_*P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}f_*Q)\ar[r]^{(4)}\ar[d]_{(12)}& d_*d^!f_*(P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Q)\ar[r]^{(5)}&d_*d^!f_*K_X\ar[d]^{(13)}\\ d_*'d'^!E\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}d_*''d''^!F\ar[r]^{(6)}& d_*d^!(E\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}F)\ar[rr]^{(7)}&&d_*d^!K_Y } \tag{4.4.2} \]

where the arrows are the following: (1) is deduced from 4.2.2 by applying \(f_*\), (2) is defined by 4.1.2, (3) is given by 4.2.2, (4) by the canonical arrow \(f_*P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}f_*Q\longrightarrow f_*(P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Q)\), (5) by 4.1.2, (6) by 4.2.2, (7) by 4.1.2, (8) is the tensor product of arrows 3.7.3, (9) and (10) are defined by 3.7.3, (11) and (12) by the isomorphisms \(f_*P\longrightarrow E\), \(f_*Q\longrightarrow F\) (3.3.1), (13) by the adjunction arrow \(f_*K_X\longrightarrow K_Y\). By definition, 4.4.1 is identified, via 3.1.1 and 3.2.1, with the outer boundary of 4.4.2. It therefore suffices to prove that the squares \(A\), \(B\), \(C\), \(D\) are commutative. The commutativity of \(B\) and \(C\) is obvious, by functoriality. It remains to prove that of \(A\) and \(D\).
a) Commutativity of \(D\). It suffices to prove that the square
\[ \xymatrix{ f_*P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}f_*Q\ar[r]\ar[d]&f_*K_X\ar[d]\\ E\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}F\ar[r]&K_Y } \tag{4.4.3} \]

(corresponding to \(D\) for \(d=\operatorname{Id}\)) commutes. Recall that the pairing \(P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Q\longrightarrow K_X\) (resp. \(E\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}F\longrightarrow K_Y\)) is the external tensor product of the evaluation arrows \(DL_i\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}L_i\longrightarrow K_{X_i}\) (resp. \(DM_i\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}M_i\longrightarrow K_{Y_i}\)). Via the Künneth isomorphisms \(f_{1*}DL_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S f_{2*}L_2\xrightarrow{\sim}f_*P\), \(f_{1*}L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S f_{2*}DL_2\xrightarrow{\sim}f_*Q\), 4.4.3 is therefore identified with the external tensor product of the squares
\[ \xymatrix{ f_{i*}DL_i\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}f_{i*}L_i\ar[r]^{(1)}\ar[d]_{(3)}&f_{i*}K_{X_i}\ar[d]^{(4)}\\ DM_i\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}M_i\ar[r]^{(2)}&K_{Y_i} } \tag{4.4.4} \]
where (2) is the evaluation arrow, (1) the composite of the canonical arrow

\(f_{i*}DL_i\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}f_{i*}L_i\longrightarrow f_{i*}(DL_i\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}L_i)\) and of the arrow defined by the evaluation arrow, (3) is the tensor product of the duality isomorphism \(f_{i*}DL_i\longrightarrow DM_i\) and of the identity arrow of \(M_i\), and (4) is the adjunction arrow. It therefore suffices to verify the commutativity of the squares 4.4.4. Since the duality isomorphism above is the composite of the canonical arrow (SGA 4 XVIII 3.1.9.5)
\[ f_{i*}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(L_i,K_{X_i})\longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_{i*}L_i,f_{i*}K_{X_i}) \]
and of the arrow deduced from the adjunction arrow \(f_{i*}K_{X_i}\longrightarrow K_{Y_i}\), it suffices to see that the following square commutes

\[ \xymatrix{ f_{i*}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(L_i,K_{X_i})\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}f_{i*}L_i \ar[r]^{(1)}\ar[d]_{(3)}&f_{i*}K_{X_i}\ar[d]^{\mathrm{Id}}\\ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_{i*}L_i,f_{i*}K_{X_i})\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}f_{i*}L_i \ar[r]^{(2)}&f_{i*}K_{X_i} } \tag{4.4.5} \]

where (1) is the arrow (1) of 4.4.4, (2) the evaluation arrow, and (3) the tensor product by \(f_{i*}L_i\) of the canonical arrow just mentioned. Now, more generally, for any arrow \(N_i\longrightarrow f_{i*}L_i\) of \(D_{ctf}(X_i)\), i.e. any arrow \(L_i\xrightarrow{u}N_i\) of type 1 above \(f_i\), the triangle

\[ \xymatrix{ f_{i*}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(L_i,K_{X_i})\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}N_i \ar[d]\ar[dr]&\\ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(N_i,f_{i*}K_{X_i})\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}N_i \ar[r]&f_{i*}K_{X_i} } \]

deduced from 4.4.5 commutes: indeed, by functoriality, we may assume that \(L_i=f_i^*N_i\), with \(u\) being given by the identity of \(L_i\); the arrow \(f_{i*}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(L_i,K_{X_i})\longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(N_i,f_{i*}K_{X_i})\) is then the inverse of the trivial duality isomorphism, and the commutativity of the triangle is verified immediately at the level of complexes, by resolving \(N_i\) flatly and \(K_{X_i}\) injectively.

b) Commutativity of \(A\). Referring back to the definition of 3.7.3, one sees that it suffices to verify this commutativity in each of the following cases: (i) \(X_i=Y_i\), \(f_i=\) the identity arrow of \(X_i\); (ii) the lateral faces of 4.4.0 are cartesian. Let us place ourselves in case (i). The square \(A\) is the outer boundary of the diagram

\[ \xymatrix{ c_*'c'^!P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}c_*''c''^!Q\ar[r]^-{\sim}\ar[d]_{(8)}& c_*(c'^!P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_Xc''^!Q)\ar[r]\ar[d]&c_*c^!(P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Q)\ar[d]^{(9)}\\ d_*'d'^!P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}d_*''d''^!Q\ar[r]^-{\sim}& d_*(d'^!P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_Xd''^!Q)\ar[r]&d_*d^!(P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Q) } \]

in which the horizontal isomorphisms of \(A_1\) are Künneth isomorphisms, the horizontal arrows of \(A_2\) are given by 4.2.1, and the middle vertical arrow is deduced by applying \(d_*\) from the arrow \(g_!(c'^!P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_Xc''^!Q)\longrightarrow d'^!P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_Xd''^!Q\) defined, via Künneth, by the external tensor product of the adjunction arrows \(f'_!c'^!P\longrightarrow d'^!P\), \(f''_!c''^!Q\longrightarrow d''^!Q\). Now these adjunction arrows and the identity arrows of \(d'^!d'^!P\), \(d''^!d''^!Q\) define commutative triangles of arrows of type 3 (1.4)
\[ \xymatrix{&c'^!P\ar[dl]\ar[d]&&&c''^!Q\ar[dl]\ar[d]\\ d'^!d'^!P&d'^!P\ar[l]&&d''^!d''^!Q&d''^!Q\ar[l]} \]
above
\[ \xymatrix{&C'\ar[dl]_{c'}\ar[d]^{f'}&&&C''\ar[dl]_{c''}\ar[d]^{f''}\\ X&D'\ar[l]^{d'}&&X&D''\ar[l]^{d''}} \]
By external tensor product (over \(X\)), these triangles provide a commutative triangle

\[ \xymatrix{&c'^!P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_Xc''^!Q\ar[dl]\ar[d]&&\text{over}&&C\ar[dl]_c\ar[d]^g\\ d'^!d'^!P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_Xd''^!d''^!Q&d'^!P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_Xd''^!Q\ar[l]&&&X&D\ar[l]^d} \]
The commutativity of this triangle is equivalent to that of the square deduced from \(A_1\) by inverting the horizontal arrows. On the other hand, we have commutative triangles of arrows of type 3
\[ \xymatrix{&c'^!P\ar[dl]\ar[d]&&&c''^!Q\ar[dl]\ar[d]\\ P&d'^!P\ar[l]&&Q&d''^!Q\ar[l]} \]
defined by the identity arrows of \(d'^!P\), \(c'^!P\), \(d''^!Q\), \(c''^!Q\). By external tensor product, we deduce from them a commutative triangle
\[ \xymatrix{&c'^!P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_Xc''^!Q\ar[dl]\ar[d]\\ P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Q&d'^!P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_Xd''^!Q\ar[l]} \]
which can be interpreted as a morphism of arrows of type 3 above \(g:C\longrightarrow D\):
\[ \xymatrix{ c'^!P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_Xc''^!Q\ar[r]\ar[d]&c^!(P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Q)\ar[d]\\ d'^!P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_Xd''^!Q\ar[r]&d^!(P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Q) } \]
or again as a commutative square above \(D\):

\[ \xymatrix{ g_*(c'^!P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_Xc''^!Q)\ar[r]\ar[d]&g_*c^!(P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Q)\ar[d]\\ d'^!P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_Xd''^!Q\ar[r]&d^!(P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Q) } \]

But the square deduced from the latter by applying \(d_*\) is none other than \(A_2\), whence the commutativity of \(A\) in case (i). It remains to treat case (ii). The faces of 4.4.0 containing \(f\) being cartesian, the arrows
\[ f'_*c'^!P\longrightarrow d'^!f_*P,\qquad f''_*c''^!Q\longrightarrow d''^!f_*Q \quad(3.7.3) \]

are isomorphisms. Their inverses define arrows of type 1 \(c'^!P\longrightarrow d'^!f_*P\), \(c''^!Q\longrightarrow d''^!f_*Q\) above \(f'\), \(f''\), whence, by direct image, commutative squares of arrows of type 1
\[ \xymatrix{c_*'c'^!P&c'^!P\ar[l]\ar[d]\\d_*'d'^!P&d'^!f_*P\ar[l]} \qquad \xymatrix{c_*''c''^!Q&c''^!Q\ar[l]\ar[d]\\d_*''d''^!Q&d''^!f_*Q\ar[l]} \]
above the faces of 4.4.0 containing \(f\). By tensor product, we deduce from them a commutative square of \(D_{ctf}(Y)\)

\[ \xymatrix{ d_*'d'^!f_*P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}d_*''d''^!f_*Q\ar[r]\ar[d]& d_*(d'^!f_*P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_Yd''^!f_*Q)\ar[d]\\ f_*c_*'c'^!P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}f_*c_*''c''^!Q\ar[r]& d_*g_*(c'^!P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_Xc''^!Q) } \tag{4.4.6} \]

where the left-hand vertical arrow is the inverse of (8), the horizontal arrows are given by Künneth, and the right-hand vertical arrow by external tensor product over \(Y\) of the arrows \(f'_*c'^!P\longrightarrow d'^!f_*P\), \(f''_*c''^!Q\longrightarrow d''^!f_*Q\). The same tensor product defines the left-hand vertical arrow of the square

\[ \xymatrix{ d'^!f_*P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_Yd''^!f_*Q\ar[r]\ar[d]&d^!f_*(P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Q)\ar[d]\\ g_*(c'^!P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_Xc''^!Q)\ar[r]&g_*c^!(P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Q) } \tag{4.4.7} \]

where the right-hand vertical arrow is 3.7.3, and the horizontal arrows are defined by 4.2.1. The outer boundary of the diagram composed of 4.4.6 and of \(d_*(4.4.7)\) is the square deduced from \(A\) by inverting the vertical arrows. It therefore suffices to prove that 4.4.7 commutes, in other words that the arrows 4.2.1

\[ c'^!P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_Xc''^!Q\longrightarrow c^!(P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Q), \qquad d'^!f_*P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_Yd''^!f_*Q\longrightarrow d^!f_*(P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Q) \]

define a morphism from \(c'^!P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_Xc''^!Q\longrightarrow d'^!f_*P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_Yd''^!f_*Q\) to \(c^!(P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Q)\longrightarrow d^!f_*(P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Q)\), regarded as arrows of type 1 above \(g\). By adjunction, we are reduced to verifying that the tensor products of adjunction arrows

\[ c_*'c'^!P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}c_*''c''^!Q\longrightarrow P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Q,\qquad d_*'d'^!f_*P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}d_*''d''^!f_*Q\longrightarrow f_*P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}f_*Q \]

define a morphism, between arrows of type 1 above \(f\), from the tensor product of the arrows \(c_*'c'^!P\longrightarrow d_*'d'^!f_*P\) and \(c_*''c''^!Q\longrightarrow d_*''d''^!f_*Q\) to the tensor product of the arrows \(P\longrightarrow f_*P\) and \(Q\longrightarrow f_*Q\). For this it suffices to verify that the adjunction arrows \(c_*'c'^!P\longrightarrow P\), \(d_*'d'^!f_*P\longrightarrow f_*P\) define a morphism from \(c_*'c'^!P\longrightarrow d_*'d'^!f_*P\) to \(P\longrightarrow f_*P\), as well as the analogous assertion
with \((c'',d'')\). We may restrict ourselves to the case of \((c',d')\), the statement being the same in both cases. We must see that the triangle
\[ \xymatrix{ &f'_*c'^!c'^!P\ar[dl]\ar[d]^{\sim}&\\ f_*P&d'^!d'^!f_*P\ar[l] } \]

where the horizontal and oblique arrows are given by the adjunction arrows and the vertical arrow by the isomorphism 3.7.3, is commutative. Taking into account the definition of 3.7.3 (SGA 4 XVIII 3.1.12.3) as the transpose of a base-change isomorphism, we are reduced, by applying the functor \(\operatorname{Hom}(R,-)\) (\(R\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(Y)\)), to verifying the commutativity of the square
\[ \xymatrix{ c'_*c'^!f^*R\ar[r]^{\mathrm{Id}}&c'_*f'^!d'^*R\\ f^*R\ar[u]\ar[r]&f^*d'_*d'^*R\ar[u]_{\sim} } \]

where the left-hand vertical arrow (resp. lower horizontal arrow) is defined by the adjunction arrow \(1\longrightarrow c'_*c'^*\) (resp. \(1\longrightarrow d'_*d'^*\)), and the right-hand vertical arrow by the base-change isomorphism \(f^*d'_*\xrightarrow{\sim}c'_*f'^*\). Now this square commutes by the very definition of the base-change arrow. The commutativity of \(A\) is thus established, which completes the proof of the theorem.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.5.

Under the hypotheses of 4.4, we have a commutative square

\[ \xymatrix{ \operatorname{Hom}(c_1'^*L_1,c_2'^!L_2)\otimes \operatorname{Hom}(c_2''^*L_2,c_1''^!L_1)\ar[r]^{4.2.5}\ar[d]_{3.7.6\,\otimes\,3.7.6}&H^0(C,K_C)\ar[d]^{g_*}\\ \operatorname{Hom}(d_1'^*f_{1*}L_1,d_2'^!f_{2*}L_2)\otimes \operatorname{Hom}(d_2''^*f_{2*}L_2,d_1''^!f_{1*}L_1)\ar[r]^{4.2.5}&H^0(D,K_D), } \]

where \(g_*\) is defined by the adjunction arrow \(g_*K_C=g_*g^!K_D\longrightarrow K_D\).

In other words, for \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}(c_1'^*L_1,c_2'^!L_2)\), \(v\in\operatorname{Hom}(c_2''^*L_2,c_1''^!L_1)\), we have
\[ \langle f_*u,f_*v\rangle=g_*\langle u,v\rangle. \tag{4.5.1} \]
For every proper \(S\)-scheme \(t:T\longrightarrow S\), let us denote by
\[ \int_T:H^0(T,K_T)\longrightarrow H^0(S,K_S)=H^0(S,A) \tag{4.6} \]
the arrow defined by the adjunction arrow \(t_!K_T\longrightarrow A\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.7. (Lefschetz-Verdier formula).

Under the hypotheses of 4.4, suppose that \(Y_1=Y_2=S=D'=D''\). For \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}(c_1'^*L_1,c_2'^!L_2)\), \(v\in\operatorname{Hom}(c_2''^*L_2,c_1''^!L_1)\), we have

\[ \langle u_*,v_*\rangle=\int_C\langle u,v\rangle, \tag{4.7.1} \]

where \(u_*\in\operatorname{Hom}(f_{1*}L_1,f_{2*}L_2)\), \(v_*\in\operatorname{Hom}(f_{2*}L_2,f_{1*}L_1)\) are the homomorphisms deduced from \(u,v\) by direct image on \(S\) (3.7.6), and \(\langle u_*,v_*\rangle=\operatorname{Tr}(u_*v_*)=\operatorname{Tr}(v_*u_*)\) is the cup-product, in the sense of (SGA 6 I 8.3), of the homomorphisms of perfect complexes \(u_*,v_*\) (4.1.5).
Taking 3.6.5 into account, we have in particular:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.8.

Let \(t:T\longrightarrow S\) be a proper \(S\)-scheme, \(\Delta\subset T\times_S T\) the diagonal, \(F:T\longrightarrow T\) a \(S\)-endomorphism of \(T\), \(T^F=(\text{graph of }F)\times_{(T\times_S T)}\Delta\) the scheme of fixed points of \(F\), \(L\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(T)\), \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}(F^*L,L)\) a correspondence from \(L\) to \(L\) with support in the graph of \(F\), and \(1\in\operatorname{Hom}(L,L)\) the identity correspondence, with support in \(\Delta\) (3.2 b)). Then we have

\[ \operatorname{Tr}(u_*)=\int_{T^F}\langle u,1\rangle, \]

where \(u_*\) is the endomorphism of \(t_*L\) composed of \(t_*L\longrightarrow t_*F^*L\) (inverse image) and of \(t_*u:t_*F^*L\longrightarrow t_*L\).
From the remarks of 3.2 a) and 3.6 we deduce on the other hand:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.9.

Under the hypotheses of 4.7, we suppose that \(X_i\) is smooth over \(S\), purely of relative dimension \(r_i\), and that \(C'\) (resp. \(C''\)) is a closed subscheme

of \(X\), finite and of finite tor-dimension over \(X_2\) (resp. \(X_1\)). Let \(cl(C')\in H_{C'}^{2r_1}(X,A(r_1))\), \(cl(C'')\in H_{C''}^{2r_2}(X,A(r_2))\) be the cohomology classes of \(C'\) and \(C''\), and \(cl(C')_*\in\operatorname{Hom}(Rf_{1*}A,Rf_{2*}A)\), \(cl(C'')_*\in\operatorname{Hom}(Rf_{2*}A,Rf_{1*}A)\) the corresponding homomorphisms (3.2 a), 3.7.6). We then have:
\[ \langle cl(C')_*,cl(C'')_*\rangle=\int_{C'\cap C''}cl(C)cl(D), \]
where
is the usual cup-product.
Taking 4.3 into account, we have in particular:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.10.

Under the hypotheses of 4.9, suppose that \(C'\cap C''\) is finite. For \(z\in C'\cap C''\), denote by \((C'.C'')_z\) the image in \(A\) of the intersection multiplicity of \(C'\) and \(C''\) at \(z\). Then we have

\[ \langle cl(C')_*,cl(C'')_*\rangle= \sum_{z\in C'\cap C''}(C'.C'')_z. \]
4.11. Under the hypotheses of 4.9, we have in general
\[ \langle cl(C')_*,cl(C'')_*\rangle= \sum_{Z\in\pi_0(C'\cap C'')}(cl(C')cl(C''))_Z, \tag{4.11.1} \]

where \(\pi_0(C'\cap C'')\) denotes the set of connected components of \(C'\cap C''\), and \((cl(C')cl(C''))_Z\) the restriction of \(cl(C')cl(C'')\) to \(H^0(Z,K_Z)\). When \(Z\) is a component of dimension \(\geq1\), the computation of \((cl(C')cl(C''))_Z\) poses problems, owing to the fact that one is in an « excess » situation. If \(Z\) is smooth, purely of dimension \(d\), if \(C'\) and \(C''\) are smooth in a neighborhood of \(Z\), and if \(X\) is projective, one can show that we have
where \(N_Z\) is the locally free sheaf of rank \(d\) defined by the exact sequence of normal bundles
\[ 0\longrightarrow N_{Z/C'}\oplus N_{Z/C''}\longrightarrow N_{Z/X} \longrightarrow N_Z\longrightarrow0, \]

and \(c_d(N_Z)\in H^{2d}(Z,A(d))\) denotes its \(d\)-th Chern class (SGA 5 VII) (use (SGA 6 XIV 6.3) to reduce to a computation of intersection numbers in K-theory, and apply (SGA 6 VII 2.5), cf. ([10] 4.1); the projectivity hypothesis is doubtless unnecessary).

Let \(T\) be a smooth projective \(S\)-scheme, take as correspondence \(C'\) (resp. \(C''\)) the graph of an \(S\)-endomorphism \(F\) (resp. of the diagonal), and suppose that the scheme of fixed points \(T^F=C'\cap C''\) is smooth. The sheaf \(N_{(T^F)}\) above is none other than the tangent sheaf of \(T^F\), so that 4.11.2 implies, by the Gauss-Bonnet formula (SGA 5 VII 4.9), the analogue of a formula well known to topologists:
\[ \operatorname{Tr}(F,R\Gamma(T,A))=\operatorname{EP}(T^F), \tag{4.11.3} \]
where \(\operatorname{EP}(T^F)\) denotes the Euler-Poincaré characteristic of \(T_s^F\) for \(s\) a geometric point above \(S\) (loc. cit.).
