The computation of \((cl(C')cl(C''))_Z\) for a component \(Z\) of dimension \(\geq1\) which is not smooth has not been undertaken in general, at least to the knowledge of the redactor.

4.12. In the situation of 4.7, let \(z\) be an isolated point of \(C\), with projection \(z_i\) on \(X_i\), \(a'\) (resp. \(a''\)) on \(C'\) (resp. \(C''\)), and let \(\bar z\) be a geometric point above \(z\), with image \(\bar z_i\) above \(z_i\). We suppose that \(c_2'\) (resp. \(c_1''\)) is quasi-finite and flat at \(a'\) (resp. \(a''\)), that \(L_i\) is perfect in a neighborhood of \(z_i\), and that \(u\) (resp. \(v\)) is given in a neighborhood of \(a'\) (resp. \(a''\)) by

\[ \operatorname{Tr}_{c_2'}\circ c_2'^*L_2\longrightarrow L_2 \quad\text{and}\quad s\in\operatorname{Hom}(c_1'^*L_1,c_2'^*L_2) \quad(\text{resp. }\operatorname{Tr}_{c_1''}\circ c_1''^*L_1\longrightarrow L_1 \text{ and }t\in\operatorname{Hom}(c_2''^*L_2,c_1''^*L_1)). \]

Denote by \(s_{\bar z}\in\operatorname{Hom}(L_{1\bar z_1},L_{2\bar z_2})\) (resp. \(t_{\bar z}\in\operatorname{Hom}(L_{2\bar z_2},L_{1\bar z_1})\)) the homomorphism deduced from \(s\) (resp. \(t\)) by passage to the stalks. Denote also, as in 3.2 a), by \(cl(C')\) (resp. \(cl(C'')\)) the correspondence from \(X_1\) to \(X_2\) (resp. \(X_2\) to \(X_1\)) defined, in a neighborhood of \(a'\) (resp. \(a''\)), by \(c_2'\) (resp. \(c_1''\)), and by \((C'.C'')_z\) the corresponding local term \(\langle cl(C'),cl(C'')\rangle_z\) (equal to the image in \(A\) of the
intersection multiplicity of \(C'\) and \(C''\) at \(z\) when \(C'\) and \(C''\) are closed subschemes of \(X\), with \(X_1\) and \(X_2\) smooth). We then have
\[ \langle u,v\rangle_z=(C'.C'')_z\langle s_{\bar z},t_{\bar z}\rangle. \tag{4.12.1} \]
Indeed, this follows easily from 4.2.6.

4.13. If \(T\) is a scheme over \(S\), denote by \(DF(T)\) the filtered derived category of complexes of \(A_T\)-Modules with finite filtration ([9] V 1), and by \(DF_{ctf}(T)\) the full subcategory consisting of the \(L\) such that \(\operatorname{gr}(L)\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(T)\). The sorite of compatibility of filtered derived functors with passage to the associated graded objects implies that \(DF_{ctf}\) is stable under the six operations \(f^*,f_*,f_!,f^!,\mathbin{\overset L\otimes},R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}\). The definitions and results of the preceding sections are valid, mutatis mutandis, in \(DF_{ctf}\). The pairing 4.1.2 is compatible with passage to the associated graded objects (\(K_X\) being regarded as equipped with the trivial filtration), and it follows that, with the notation of 4.2.5, we have, for \(L_i\in\operatorname{ob}DF_{ctf}(X_i)\), \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}_{DF}(c_1'^*L_1,c_2'^!L_2)\), \(v\in\operatorname{Hom}_{DF}(d_2^*L_2,d_1^!L_1)\), the following equality in \(H^0(E,K_E)\):
\[ \langle u,v\rangle=\sum_i\langle\operatorname{gr}^i u, \operatorname{gr}^i v\rangle. \tag{4.13.1} \]

As Deligne observes, this remark applies in particular to the situation considered by Langlands in ([13] §7). The correspondences he considers in (loc. cit. 7.11) indeed preserve, near the fixed points, finite filtrations on the sheaves whose associated graded pieces induce on the strict localization at the image of a fixed point either a constant sheaf or a constant sheaf on the complement of the closed point extended by zero. The computation of the local terms reduces easily, by 4.13, to 4.12.1 and to the case of sheaves concentrated on the closed points that are images of the fixed points, a case which is treated directly. Applying 4.7, we obtain the Lefschetz formula required in ([13] 7.11). Let us point out that the general statement (loc. cit. 7.12) is true; the proof, which is more delicate, will be given in III B.
5. Complements
5.1. Dual of a correspondence

Let, for \(i=1,2\), \(X_i\) be an \(S\)-scheme, \(X=X_1\times_S X_2\), \(p_i:X\longrightarrow X_i\) the projections, \(L_i\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(X_i)\). Every cohomological correspondence \(u\) from \(L_1\) to \(L_2\) gives, by application of \(D_X\) and identification of \(Dp_2^!\) with \(p_2^*D\), and of \(Dp_1^*\) with \(p_1^!D\), a correspondence \(Du\) from \(DL_2\) to \(DL_1\), called the dual of \(u\). In other words, \(D\) defines a homomorphism
\[ D:\operatorname{Hom}(p_1^*L_1,p_2^!L_2)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(p_2^*DL_2,p_1^!DL_1). \tag{5.1.1} \]
We verify that 5.1.1 arises, by application of \(H^0(X,-)\), from the isomorphism
\[ D:R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(p_1^*L_1,p_2^!L_2)\longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(p_2^*DL_2,p_1^!DL_1) \tag{5.1.2} \]
making the following square commutative

\[ \xymatrix{ p_1^*DL_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}p_2^*L_2 \ar[r]^{3.1.1}\ar[d] & R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(p_1^*L_1,p_2^!L_2)\ar[d]\\ p_2^*DDL_2\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}p_1^*DL_1 \ar[r]^{3.1.1} & R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(p_2^*DL_2,p_1^!DL_1), } \tag{5.1.3} \]

where the left vertical arrow is the composite of the symmetry isomorphism and of the one defined by the biduality isomorphism \(L_2\longrightarrow DDL_2\). It follows easily from the definitions that the square below is commutative

\[ \xymatrix{ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(L_1,L_2)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(L_2,L_1)\ar[r]^{4.1.3}\ar[d]^{D\otimes D} & K_X\ar[d]^{\mathrm{Id}}\\ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(DL_2,DL_1)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(DL_1,DL_2)\ar[r]^{4.1.3} & K_X. } \tag{5.1.4} \]
Let \(c:C\longrightarrow X\) be an \(S\)-morphism, and set \(c_i=p_ic\) ; we deduce from 5.1.2, by application of \(c_*c^!\) (3.2.1), an isomorphism
\[ D:\operatorname{Hom}(c_1^*L_1,c_2^!L_2)\xrightarrow{\sim} \operatorname{Hom}(c_2^*DL_2,c_1^!DL_1), \tag{5.1.5} \]
which we verify is identified with the one given by application of the functor \(D_X\) and

the identifications of \(Dc_1^*\) with \(c_1^!D\) and of \(Dc_2^!\) with \(c_2^*D\). If \(c':C'\longrightarrow X\), \(c'':C''\longrightarrow X\) are \(S\)-morphisms, it follows from 5.1.4 that we have, for \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}(c_1'^*L_1,c_2'^!L_2)\), \(v\in\operatorname{Hom}(c_2''^*L_2,c_1''^!L_1)\),
\[ \langle u,v\rangle=\langle Du,Dv\rangle, \tag{5.1.6} \]
with the notation of 4.2.5.
We verify on the other hand that, in the situation of 3.3, the following square is commutative

\[ \xymatrix{ f_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(L_1,L_2)\ar[r]^{3.3.1}\ar[d]^{f_*D} & R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(f_{1!}L_1,f_{2*}L_2)\ar[d]^D\\ f_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(DL_2,DL_1)\ar[r]^{3.3.1} & R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(f_{2!}DL_2,f_{1*}DL_1), } \tag{5.1.7} \]
and consequently that, in the situation of 3.7.6, we have
5.2. Composition of correspondences

Let, for \(i=1,2,3\), \(X_i\) be an \(S\)-scheme, \(X_{ij}=X_i\times_S X_j\), \(X=X_1\times_S X_2\times_S X_3\), \(p_i:X\longrightarrow X_i\), \(p_{ij}:X\longrightarrow X_{ij}\) the projections, \(L_i\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(X_i)\). We have \(X=X_{12}\times_{X_2}X_{23}\), and

\[ (DL_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_2)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{X_2} (DL_2\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_3) =p_1^*DL_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}p_2^*L_2 \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}p_2^*DL_2\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}p_3^*L_3. \]
The evaluation arrow \(L_2\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}DL_2\longrightarrow K_{X_2}\) therefore defines a pairing
\[ (DL_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_2)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{X_2} (DL_2\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_3)\longrightarrow p_{13}^!(DL_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_3), \tag{5.2.1} \]

the right-hand side being identified with \((DL_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_3)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S K_{X_2}\) by Künneth (1.7.3). Taking 3.1.1, 3.1.2 into account, we can rewrite 5.2.1 in the form

\[ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(L_1,L_2)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{X_2} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(L_2,L_3)\longrightarrow p_{13}^!R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(L_1,L_3) =R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(p_1^*L_1,p_3^!L_3), \tag{5.2.2} \]
the identification on the right-hand side being given by the induction isomorphism (SGA 4 XVIII 3.1.12.2)), whence a pairing, called composition of correspondences,
\[ \operatorname{Hom}_S(L_1,L_2)\otimes\operatorname{Hom}_S(L_2,L_3) \longrightarrow\operatorname{Hom}(p_1^*L_1,p_3^!L_3), \tag{5.2.3} \]

which we will denote \((u,v)\longmapsto vu\). We verify that, if \(p_{ij,i}:X_{ij}\longrightarrow X_i\) and \(p_{ij,j}:X_{ij}\longrightarrow X_j\) denote the projections, 5.2.2 can also be obtained by composition of the arrow

\[ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(L_1,L_2)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{X_2} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(L_2,L_3)\longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(p_{12}^*L_1,p_{12}^*p_{12,2}^!L_2) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(p_{23}^*p_{23,2}^*L_2,p_{23}^!L_3) \]

(tensor product of the canonical arrows \(p_{12}^*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(L_1,L_2)\longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(p_1^*L_1,p_{12}^*p_{12,2}^!L_2)\) and \(p_{23}^*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(L_2,L_3)\longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(p_{23}^*p_{23,2}^*L_2,p_3^!L_3)\)), of the arrow defined by the base change arrow \(p_{12}^*p_{12,2}^!L_2\longrightarrow p_{23}^!p_{23,2}^*L_2\), and of a usual composition arrow on the \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}\).

Let \(c':C'\longrightarrow X_{12}\), \(c'':C''\longrightarrow X_{23}\) be correspondences, and \(c:C=C'\times_{X_2}C''\longrightarrow X\) the "composite" correspondence. From 5.2.2 we deduce (or we define directly, by a composition analogous to the one just described) a pairing

\[ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(c_1'^*L_1,c_2'^!L_2) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{X_2} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(c_2''^*L_2,c_3''^!L_3) \longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(c_1^*L_1,c_3^!L_3), \tag{5.2.4} \]
whence a "composition" pairing \((u,v)\longmapsto vu\) :
\[ \operatorname{Hom}(c_1'^*L_1,c_2'^!L_2)\otimes \operatorname{Hom}(c_2''^*L_2,c_3''^!L_3)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(c_1^*L_1,c_3^!L_3). \tag{5.2.5} \]

We verify that the composition of correspondences is compatible with direct images, in the following sense. Let, for \(1\leq i\leq3\), \(f_i:X_i\longrightarrow Y_i\) be an \(S\)-morphism, with \(f_2\) proper, \(c',c'',c\) morphisms as above, with \(c'\) and \(c''\) proper, \(d':D'\longrightarrow Y_{12}\), \(d'':D''\longrightarrow Y_{23}\) proper morphisms, \(d:D=D'\times_{Y_2}D''\longrightarrow Y\), and commutative squares
\[ \xymatrix{X_{12}&C'\ar[l]\ar[d]\\Y_{12}\ar[u]^{f_{12}}&D'\ar[l]} \qquad \xymatrix{X_{23}&C''\ar[l]\ar[d]\\Y_{23}\ar[u]^{f_{23}}&D''\ar[l]}. \]
We then have a commutative square

\[ \xymatrix{ \operatorname{Hom}(c_1'^*L_1,c_2'^!L_2)\otimes \operatorname{Hom}(c_2''^*L_2,c_3''^!L_3)\ar[r]^{5.2.5}\ar[d]^{f_{12*}\otimes f_{23*}} & \operatorname{Hom}(c_1^*L_1,c_3^!L_3)\ar[d]^{f_{13*}}\\ \operatorname{Hom}(d_1'^*M_1,d_2'^!M_2)\otimes \operatorname{Hom}(d_2''^*M_2,d_3''^!M_3)\ar[r]^{5.2.5} & \operatorname{Hom}(d_1^*M_1,d_3^!M_3), } \tag{5.2.6} \]
where \(M_1=f_{1!}L_1\), \(M_2=f_{2*}L_2\), \(M_3=f_{3*}M_3\), and an analogous commutative square with \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}\)'s, which the reader will write out.
Finally, suppose that \(X_1=X_3\), \(L_1=L_3\). The fundamental pairing 4.1.2 can be interpreted as the composite of the pairing

\[ q_1^*DL_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}q_2^*L_2 \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}q_2^*DL_2\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}q_1^*L_1 \longrightarrow q_1^*DL_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}q_2^*K_{X_2} \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}q_1^*L_1 \tag{5.2.7} \]

defined by \(L_2\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}DL_2\longrightarrow K_{X_2}\), or, what amounts to the same thing, induced by 5.2.1 on \(X_{12}\) via the diagonal \(X_{12}\longrightarrow X_1\times_S X_2\times_S X_1\) (\(q_i:X_{12}\longrightarrow X_i\) denoting the projection), and of the pairing
\[ q_1^*DL_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}q_2^*K_{X_2} \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}q_1^*L_1\longrightarrow K_{X_{12}} \tag{5.2.8} \]

defined by \(DL_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}L_1\longrightarrow K_{X_1}\). It follows that, for \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}_S(L_1,L_2)\), \(v\in\operatorname{Hom}_S(L_2,L_1)\), we have, in \(H^0(X_1\times_S X_2,K_{X_1\times_S X_2})\),
\[ \langle u,v\rangle=\langle vu,1\rangle, \tag{5.2.9} \]

where \(1\) denotes the identity correspondence from \(L_1\) to \(L_1\) on \(X_1\times_S X_1\), with support in the diagonal (3.2 b)), and the cup-product on the right-hand side is taken in the sense of 4.2.5, taking into account the fact that the fiber product \(X\times_{(X_1\times_S X_1)}X_1\) (by \(p_{13}:X\longrightarrow X_{13}=X_1\times_SX_1\) and the diagonal \(X_1\longrightarrow X_1\times_SX_1\)) is identified with \(X_1\times_SX_2\). We see similarly, more generally, that, if \(c':C'\longrightarrow X_{12}\), \(c'':C''\longrightarrow X_{21}\) are correspondences, \(c:C=C'\times_{X_2}C''\longrightarrow X_1\times_SX_2\times_SX_1\) the

composite correspondence, and \(d:C'\times_{X_{12}}C''=D\longrightarrow X_{12}\) the intersection correspondence, we have, for \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}(c_1'^*L_1,c_2'^!L_2)\), \(v\in\operatorname{Hom}(c_2''^*L_2,c_1''^!L_1)\), the following equality in \(H^0(D,K_D)\)
\[ \langle u,v\rangle=\langle vu,1\rangle, \tag{5.2.10} \]

where \(1\) denotes as above the diagonal correspondence, and \(D\) is identified with the fiber product of \(C\longrightarrow X_1\times_SX_1\) and the diagonal \(X_1\longrightarrow X_1\times_SX_1\). Formulas 5.2.9 and 5.2.10 generalize a well-known formula in the case of constant coefficients ([12] 1.3.6 (ii) c)).
5.3. External tensor product of correspondences

Let, for \(1\leq i\leq4\), \(X_i\) be an \(S\)-scheme, \(X=X_1\times_SX_2\times_SX_3\times_SX_4\), \(X_{ij}=X_i\times_SX_j\), \(L_i\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(X_i)\). The Künneth isomorphism (1.7.6, 2.2.4)
\[ DL_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S DL_3\xrightarrow{\sim} D(L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_3) \]
defines an isomorphism on \(X\)

\[ (DL_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_2)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S (DL_3\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_4)\xrightarrow{\sim} D(L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_3)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S (L_2\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_4), \tag{5.3.1} \]
which, by 3.1.1, we can rewrite in the form

\[ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(L_1,L_2)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(L_3,L_4)\xrightarrow{\sim} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_3, L_2\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_4). \tag{5.3.2} \]
Whence a pairing, called the external tensor product,
\[ \operatorname{Hom}_S(L_1,L_2)\otimes\operatorname{Hom}_S(L_3,L_4) \longrightarrow\operatorname{Hom}_S(L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_3, L_2\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_4), \tag{5.3.3} \]
which we will denote \((u,v)\longmapsto u\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S v\). The pairing 5.3.2 can also be interpreted as the composite of the external tensor product

\[ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E_1,E_2)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E_3,E_4)\xrightarrow{\sim} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S E_3, E_2\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S E_4) \]

(where \(E_1=p_{12,1}^*L_1\), etc., with obvious notations) and of the isomorphism defined by Künneth \(E_2\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S E_4\xrightarrow{\sim} p_{24}^!(L_2\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_4)\) (1.7.3).

Let \(c':C'\longrightarrow X_{12}\), \(c'':C''\longrightarrow X_{34}\) be correspondences, and \(c:C=C'\times_SC''\longrightarrow X=X_{13}\times_SX_{24}\) the product correspondence. From 5.3.2 we deduce, or we define in an analogous manner, a pairing

\[ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(c_1'^*L_1,c_2'^!L_2) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(c_3''^*L_3,c_4''^!L_4) \longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(c_{13}^*(L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_3), c_{24}^!(L_2\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_4)), \tag{5.3.4} \]
whence an external tensor product pairing, \((u,v)\longmapsto u\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S v\),

\[ \operatorname{Hom}(c_1'^*L_1,c_2'^!L_2)\otimes \operatorname{Hom}(c_3''^*L_3,c_4''^!L_4) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(c_{13}^*(L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_3), c_{24}^!(L_2\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_4)). \tag{5.3.5} \]

We verify that these pairings are compatible with direct images, in the following sense. Let, for \(1\leq i\leq4\), \(f_i:X_i\longrightarrow Y_i\) be a \(S\)-morphism, \(c',c'',c\) as above, with \(c'\) and \(c''\) proper, \(d':D'\longrightarrow Y_{12}\), \(d'':D''\longrightarrow Y_{34}\) proper morphisms, \(d:D=D'\times_SD''\longrightarrow Y=Y_{12}\times_SY_{34}\), and commutative squares
\[ \xymatrix{X_{12}&C'\ar[l]\ar[d]\\Y_{12}\ar[u]^{f_{12}}&D'\ar[l]} \qquad \xymatrix{X_{34}&C''\ar[l]\ar[d]\\Y_{34}\ar[u]^{f_{34}}&D''\ar[l]}. \]
We then have a commutative square

\[ \xymatrix{ \operatorname{Hom}(c_1'^*L_1,c_2'^!L_2)\otimes \operatorname{Hom}(c_3''^*L_3,c_4''^!L_4)\ar[r]^{5.3.5}\ar[d]^{f_{12*}\otimes f_{34*}} & \operatorname{Hom}(c_{13}^*L_{13},c_{24}^!L_{24})\ar[d]^{f_{13,24*}}\\ \operatorname{Hom}(d_1'^*M_1,d_2'^!M_2)\otimes \operatorname{Hom}(d_3''^*M_3,d_4''^!M_4)\ar[r]^{5.3.5} & \operatorname{Hom}(d_{13}^*M_{13},d_{24}^!M_{24}), } \tag{5.3.6} \]

where \(M_i=f_{i!}L_i\) if \(i=1,3\) and \(f_{i*}L_i\) otherwise, \(L_{ij}=L_i\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_j\), \(M_{ij}=M_i\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S M_j\). (We also have an analogous square with \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}\)'s).

Suppose that \(X_3=X_2\), \(X_4=X_1\), \(L_3=L_2\), \(L_4=L_1\). An argument analogous to that of the last paragraph of 5.2 shows that, for \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}_S(L_1,L_2)\), \(v\in\operatorname{Hom}_S(L_2,L_1)\), we have
\[ \langle u,v\rangle=\langle u\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S v,1\rangle, \tag{5.3.7} \]

where (1) denotes the diagonal correspondence from \(L_{12}\) to \(L_{12}\) on \(X_{12}\), and the cup-product on the right-hand side is taken in the sense of 4.2.5. More generally, if \(c':C'\longrightarrow X_{12}\), \(c'':C''\longrightarrow X_{21}\) are correspondences, their "intersection" \(C=C'\times_{X_{12}}C''\) is identified with the fiber product of \(C'\times_SC''\longrightarrow X=X_{12}\times_SX_{21}\) and of the diagonal \(X_{12}\longrightarrow X\), and we have, for \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}(c_1'^*L_1,c_2'^!L_2)\), \(v\in\operatorname{Hom}(c_2''^*L_2,c_1''^!L_1)\), the following equality in \(H^0(C,K_C)\)
\[ \langle u,v\rangle=\langle u\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S v,1\rangle, \tag{5.3.8} \]
where (1) denotes as above the diagonal correspondence. We therefore have two "diagonal" procedures (5.2.10, 5.3.8) for the computation of the "Verdier local term" \(\langle u,v\rangle\).

pages.title: 6. Appendix. Lefschetz formula for coherent sheaves.

blocks.html: 6. Appendix. Lefschetz formula for coherent sheaves.

In this section, \(S\) denotes a noetherian scheme. The \(S\)-schemes considered will be assumed separated and of finite type. Recall that, by Nagata, every \(S\)-morphism between such schemes is compactifiable, i.e. factors as an open immersion followed by a proper morphism. For every \(S\)-scheme \(X\), we will denote by \(D(X)\) the derived category of that of \(\mathcal O_X\)-Modules.

6.1 Recall ([8] App.) that for every \(S\)-morphism \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\), we have a functor \(f^!:D^+(Y)\longrightarrow D^+(X)\) (and transitivity isomorphisms \((gf)^!=f^!g^!\) for a composite (gf), with cocycle condition). For \(f\) proper, \(f^!\) is a right "adjoint" of the functor \(f_*:D^+(X)_{\mathrm{coh}}\longrightarrow D^+(Y)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) (the index "coh" denoting the full subcategory formed of complexes with coherent cohomology), this adjunction being made precise by an isomorphism
\[ f_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,f^!F)\xrightarrow{\sim} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_*E,F) \tag{6.1.1} \]

for \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(X)_{\mathrm{coh}}\), \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(Y)\), defined in a manner analogous to the isomorphism (SGA 4 XVIII 3.1.9.6). The considerations of ([8] App.5), combined with the duality theorem for projective space, show that, if \(f\) is smooth, purely of dimension \(d\), we have a canonical isomorphism
\[ f^!L\xleftarrow{\sim}f^*L\otimes\Omega^d_{X/Y}[d] ; \tag{6.1.2} \]
if moreover \(f\) is proper, the arrow
\[ \operatorname{Tr}_f:f_*(f^*L\otimes\Omega^d_{X/Y}[d])\longrightarrow L \tag{6.1.3} \]
deduced, via 6.1.2, from the adjunction arrow \(f_*f^!\longrightarrow I\), coincides with the trace morphism ([8] VI).

6.2 Contrary to what happens in the case of discrete coefficients, there is here no reasonable subcategory of \(D\) stable under "the six operations" \((f^*,f_*,f^!,f_!,\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}, R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}})\). The most natural notion of finiteness seems to be that of relative perfection (SGA 6 III 4). If \(X\) is an \(S\)-scheme, and \(L\in\operatorname{ob}D(X)\),

recall that we say that \(L\) is perfect rel. to \(S\) if \(L\) is of finite tor-dimension rel. to \(S\) and has coherent cohomology. We will denote by \(D(X)_{\mathrm{parf}/S}\) the full subcategory of \(D(X)\) formed of the complexes perfect rel. to \(S\). It is in general stable neither under \(\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}\), nor under \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}\). However, if \(E\) is perfect (in the absolute sense), and \(F\) perfect rel. to \(S\), then \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F)\) is perfect rel. to \(S\) (SGA 6 III 4.9). On the other hand, let, for \(i=1,2\), \(X_i\) be an \(S\)-scheme, \(L_i\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(X_i)\). We will set, as in 1.5,
\[ L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_2=Lp_1^*L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Lp_2^*L_2, \]

where \(p_i:X_1\times_SX_2\longrightarrow X_i\) is the projection. The consideration of this object is reasonable only if \(X_1\) and \(X_2\) are tor-independent over \(S\), i.e. (SGA 6 III 1.5) if \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^S(\mathcal O_{X_1},\mathcal O_{X_2})=0\) for \(i\ne0\). Suppose this is the case: then, if \(L_1\) and \(L_2\) are perfect rel. to \(S\), the same is true of \(L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_2\) (SGA 6 III 4.7).

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be an \(S\)-morphism. If \(f\) is proper, we have \(f_*D(X)_{\mathrm{parf}/S}\subset D(Y)_{\mathrm{parf}/S}\) (SGA 6 III 4.8). If \(f\) is of finite tor-dimension, we have \(f^*D(Y)_{\mathrm{parf}/S}\subset D(X)_{\mathrm{parf}/S}\) (SGA 6 III 4.5), and \(f^!D(Y)_{\mathrm{parf}/S}\subset D(X)_{\mathrm{parf}/S}\) (SGA 6 III 4.9).
For every \(S\)-scheme \(a:X\longrightarrow S\), we will set
\[ K_X=a^!\mathcal O_S, \tag{6.2.1} \]

and will denote by \(D_X\) (or simply \(D\)) the functor \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(-,K_X)\). If \(X\) is of finite tor-dimension over \(S\), \(K_X\) is perfect rel. to \(S\) and, for every \(L\in\operatorname{ob}D(X)_{\mathrm{parf}/S}\), we have \(DL\in\operatorname{ob}D(X)_{\mathrm{parf}/S}\), and the canonical arrow \(L\longrightarrow DDL\) is an isomorphism (SGA 6 III 4.9).

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be an \(S\)-morphism, \(L\in\operatorname{ob}D(X)\), \(M\in\operatorname{ob}D(Y)\). If \(f^*M\) is defined (for example if \(M\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(Y)\), or if \(f\) is of finite tor-dimension), we will set
\[ \operatorname{Hom}_f^{(1)}(L,M)=\operatorname{Hom}(f^*M,L),\qquad \operatorname{Hom}_f^{(2)}(L,M)=\operatorname{Hom}(L,f^*M). \]
If \(f^!M\) is defined (for example if \(M\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(Y)\), or if \(f\) is smooth, or finite
and of finite tor-dimension, or a composite of such morphisms, for in these cases \(f^!\) extends, by 6.1.1, 6.1.2, to a functor defined on the whole category \(D(Y)\)), we will set
\[ \operatorname{Hom}_f^{(3)}(L,M)=\operatorname{Hom}(L,f^!M),\qquad \operatorname{Hom}_f^{(4)}(L,M)=\operatorname{Hom}(f^!M,L). \]

The elements of \(\operatorname{Hom}_f^{(i)}(L,M)\) will be called, as in 1.4, arrows of type (i) from \(L\) to \(M\) over \(f\) (or \(f\)-arrows of type (i) from \(L\) to \(M\)). We leave to the reader the task of making explicit the composition of arrows of type (i), for fixed (i), and the corresponding fibered and cofibered categories. Let us recall only the partial adjunction formulas \(\operatorname{Hom}_f^{(1)}(L,M)=\operatorname{Hom}(M,f_*L)\) (for \(M\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(Y)\), \(L\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(X)\)), \(\operatorname{Hom}_f^{(3)}(L,M)=\operatorname{Hom}(f_*L,M)\) (for \(f\) proper, \(L\in\operatorname{ob}D(X)_{\mathrm{coh}}\), \(M\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(Y)\)).

6.3 In the situation of 1.6, suppose \(X_1\) and \(X_2\) tor-independent over \(S\), as well as \(Y_1\) and \(Y_2\). Then, for \(L_i\in\operatorname{ob}D(X_i)_{\mathrm{parf}/S}\), the Künneth arrow 1.6.4 is defined (use the fact that finite relative tor-dimension is preserved by direct image (SGA 6 III 3.7.1)) and is an isomorphism (to verify this, we may assume \(S\) and \(Y_i\) affine, and, for a Čech-type computation, we reduce to assuming also \(X_1\) and \(X_2\) affine; the conclusion is then a trivial consequence of the tor-independence hypotheses). On the other hand, for \(M_i\in\operatorname{ob}D(Y_i)_{\mathrm{parf}/S}\), with \(f_1^!M_1\) or \(f_2^!M_2\) in \(D^b\), the Künneth arrow 1.7.3 is defined(*), and is an isomorphism when \(f_1\) and \(f_2\) are of finite tor-dimension, as one verifies easily with the help of the Künneth isomorphism of type 1.6.4 just indicated, combined with 6.1.2, and 6.1.1 in the case where \(f_i\) is a closed immersion. In particular, if \(X_1\) and \(X_2\) are of finite tor-dimension over \(S\) and tor-independent, 1.7.6 is an isomorphism.

We define as in 2.1 \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(u,v)\) for \((u,v)\) of type (2,1) (resp. (3,4), with \(f\) proper in order to avoid the pro-objects due to a \(f_!\)). Under suitable degree hypotheses (see for example ([9] V 2.3)), the evaluation arrows (2.2.2) and tensor product arrows (2.2.3, 2.2.4) are defined, and the arrows
2.2.3, 2.2.4 (provided that they are defined) are isomorphisms as soon as \(E_1\) and \(E_2\) are perfect (in the absolute sense).

In the situation of 2.4, suppose the pairs \((X_1,X_2)\), \((X_1,Y_2)\), \((Y_1,X_2)\), \((Y_1,Y_2)\) tor-independent over \(S\), \(f_1\) proper, \(E_i\), \(F_i\), \(P_i\), \(Q_i\), \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E_i,F_i)\), \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(P_i,Q_i)\) perfect rel. to \(S\), and the Künneth arrows \[ f_1^!Q_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S Q_2\longrightarrow (f_1\times_S Y_2)^!(Q_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S Q_2),\qquad f_1^!Q_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S F_2\longrightarrow (f_1\times_S Y_2)^!(Q_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S F_2) \] isomorphisms. Then 2.4.1 and the diagram of 2.5 are defined, and the latter is commutative (however the horizontal arrows are not necessarily isomorphisms). The verification is the same as for 2.5.

6.4 Let, for \(i=1,2\), \(X_i\) be an \(S\)-scheme, \(X=X_1\times_SX_2\), \(p_i:X\longrightarrow X_i\), \(q_i:X_i\longrightarrow S\) the canonical projections. We assume \(X_1\) and \(X_2\) tor-independent, and \(q_1\) of finite tor-dimension. Let on the other hand \(L_i\in\operatorname{ob}D(X_i)_{\mathrm{parf}/S}\). By the analogue of 2.2.4, we have a canonical arrow

\[ DL_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_2\longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S\mathcal O_{X_2}, q_1^!\mathcal O_S\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_2), \tag{6.4.1} \]
whence, by composing with the Künneth isomorphism (6.3) \(q_1^!\mathcal O_S\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_2\xrightarrow{\sim}p_2^!L_2\), an arrow
\[ DL_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_S L_2\longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(p_1^*L_1,p_2^!L_2). \tag{6.4.2} \]
The arrow 6.4.1 (hence also 6.4.2) is an isomorphism, as we see by reducing to the case where \(q_1\) is a closed immersion.
As in 3.1.2, we will set
\[ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(p_1^*L_1,p_2^!L_2) =R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(L_1,L_2),\quad \operatorname{Hom}(p_1^*L_1,p_2^!L_2)=\operatorname{Hom}_S(L_1,L_2), \tag{6.4.3} \]

and will call the elements of \(\operatorname{Hom}_S(L_1,L_2)\) cohomological correspondences from \(L_1\) to \(L_2\). If \(c:C\longrightarrow X\) is a correspondence (proper, in practice), we have by the induction formula ([8] III 8.8)(*)
\[ c^!R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(p_1^*L_1,p_2^!L_2) =R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(c_1^*L_1,c_2^!L_2), \tag{6.4.4} \]

and the elements of \(\operatorname{Hom}(c_1^*L_1,c_2^!L_2)=H^0(X,c_*c^!R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}} (p_1^*L_1,p_2^!L_2))\) will be called (cohomological) correspondences from \(L_1\) to \(L_2\) with support in \(C\). We will see further on examples analogous to those of 3.2.

6.5 Let, for \(i=1,2\), \(f_i:X_i\longrightarrow Y_i\) be a proper \(S\)-morphism, \(f=f_1\times_Sf_2:X\longrightarrow Y\). We assume \(X_i\) and \(Y_i\) of finite tor-dimension over \(S\), and the squares of 2.4.0 tor-independent. Let \(L_i\in\operatorname{ob}D(X_i)_{\mathrm{parf}/S}\). We define as in 3.3.1 an isomorphism
\[ f_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(L_1,L_2)\xrightarrow{\sim} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(f_{1*}L_1,f_{2*}L_2), \tag{6.5.1} \]
whence, by application of \(R\Gamma(Y,-)\), an isomorphism
\[ f_*:\operatorname{Hom}_S(L_1,L_2)\xrightarrow{\sim} \operatorname{Hom}_S(f_{1*}L_1,f_{2*}L_2), \tag{6.5.2} \]

which we will denote simply \(u\longmapsto u_*\) when \(Y_1=Y_2=S\). We have a commutative diagram analogous to 3.4.1, as follows from the analogue of 2.5 pointed out in 6.3. From it we deduce a corollary analogous to 6.5, and, in the case where \(Y_1=Y_2=S\) is the spectrum of a field, a description, analogous to that of 3.6, of the operations \(u_*\) on cohomology.
Given a commutative square 3.7.1, with \(c\) proper, we have, for \(K\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(C)\) a canonical arrow
\[ f_*c_*c^!K\longrightarrow d_*d^!f_*K, \tag{6.5.3} \]

defined in the same manner as 3.7.3 (the isomorphism \(g_*c^!\xrightarrow{\sim}d^!f_*\) is valid in the present context as well: definition of the arrow by transposition of a base change arrow, and verification of the fact that it is an isomorphism by reduction to the cases where \(d\) is smooth and is a closed immersion). Taking \(K=R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(L_1,L_2)\), we deduce from 6.5.3 and 6.5.1 canonical arrows

\[ f_*c_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(c^*L_1,c^!L_2)\longrightarrow d_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(d^*f_{1*}L_1,d^!f_{2*}L_2), \tag{6.5.4} \] \[ f_*:\operatorname{Hom}(c^*L_1,c^!L_2)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(d^*f_{1*}L_1,d^!f_{2*}L_2), \tag{6.5.5} \]
analogous to 3.7.5, 3.7.6.

6.6 Let, for \(i=1,2\), \(X_i\) be \(S\)-schemes of finite tor-dimension and tor-independent, and \(L_i\in\operatorname{ob}D(X_i)_{\mathrm{parf}/S}\). The external tensor product of the evaluation arrows \(DL_i\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}L_i\longrightarrow K_{X_i}\) defines, as in 4.1, "cup-product" pairings analogous to 4.1.2, 4.1.3, 4.1.4.
Let \(c:C\longrightarrow X\), \(d:D\longrightarrow X\) be proper morphisms, \(e=c\times_Xd:E\longrightarrow X\), \(P,Q\in\operatorname{ob}D(X)_{\mathrm{parf}/S}\). By 6.1.1, we have
\[ c_*c^!P=R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(Rc_*\mathcal O_C,P) \]
(and an analogous isomorphism with \(d\)). Assuming \(c\) and \(d\) of finite tor-dimension at the points above \(e(E)\), we deduce from this, by tensor product, a canonical arrow
\[ c_*c^!P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}d_*d^!Q\longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(Rc_*\mathcal O_C\mathbin{\overset L\otimes} Rd_*\mathcal O_D,P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Q). \tag{6.6.1} \]

When \(c\) and \(d\) are tor-independent, the right-hand side can be rewritten, by Künneth and 6.1.1, as \(e_*e^!(P\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Q)\), and 6.6.1 takes the form 4.2.3. For \(P=DL_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_SL_2\), \(Q=L_1\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_SDL_2\), we deduce from 6.6.1 and the pairings indicated above pairings analogous to 4.2.4, 4.2.5:

\[ c_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(c_1^*L_1,c_2^!L_2) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}d_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(d_2^*L_2,d_1^!L_1) \longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(Rc_*\mathcal O_C \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Rd_*\mathcal O_D,K_X), \tag{6.6.2} \] \[ \langle\ ,\ \rangle:\operatorname{Hom}(c_1^*L_1,c_2^!L_2)\otimes \operatorname{Hom}(d_2^*L_2,d_1^!L_1)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(Rc_*\mathcal O_C\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Rd_*\mathcal O_D,K_X). \tag{6.6.3} \]

Just like the pairings of 4.2, these are compatible with étale localization. When \(c\) and \(d\) are tor-independent, the right-hand side of 6.6.2 (resp. 6.6.3) is canonically identified with \(e_*K_E\) (resp. \(H^0(E,K_E)\)).
Theorem 4.4 has the following analogue:

Theorem 6.7 – Let, for \(i=1,2\), \(f_i:X_i\longrightarrow Y_i\) be a proper \(S\)-morphism, \(f=f_1\times_Sf_2:X\longrightarrow Y\), \(p_i:X\longrightarrow X_i\), \(q_i:Y\longrightarrow Y_i\) the projections. Suppose given on the other hand a cube 4.4.0 with cartesian horizontal faces, with \(c'\), \(c''\), \(d'\), \(d''\) proper, and \(c'\), \(c''\) (resp. \(d'\), \(d''\)) of finite tor-dimension at the points above \(c(C)\) (resp. \(d(D)\)). We set \(c_i'=p_ic'\), \(c_i''=p_ic''\), \(d_i'=q_id'\), \(d_i''=p_id''\). Finally let \(L_i\in\operatorname{ob}D(X_i)_{\mathrm{parf}/S}\). We assume the pairs \((X_1,X_2)\), \((X_1,Y_2)\), \((Y_1,X_2)\), \((Y_1,Y_2)\) tor-independent over \(S\), and \(X_i\) and \(Y_i\) of finite tor-dimension over \(S\). We then have a commutative square

\[ \begin{CD} f_*c_*'R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(c_1'^*L_1,c_2'^!L_2)\otimes f_*c_*''R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(c_2''^*L_2,c_1''^!L_1) @>{(1)}>> f_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(Rc'_*\mathcal O_{C'}\otimes Rc''_*\mathcal O_{C''},K_X)\\ @V{(2)}VV @VV{(4)}V\\ d_*'R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(d_1'^*M_1,d_2'^!M_2)\otimes d_*''R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(d_2''^*M_2,d_1''^!M_1) @>{(3)}>> R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(Rd'_*\mathcal O_{D'}\otimes Rd''_*\mathcal O_{D''},K_Y), \end{CD} \tag{6.7.1} \]

where \(M_i=f_{i*}L_i\), (3) is given by 6.6.2, (1) is deduced from 6.6.2 by application of \(f_*\) and composition with the canonical arrow \(f_*c_*'\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}f_*c_*''\longrightarrow f_*(c_*'\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}c_*'')\), (2) is the tensor product of the arrows 6.5.4 relative to the faces containing \(f\), and (4) is the composite of the duality isomorphism 6.1.1 and of the arrow defined by the canonical arrow \[ Rd'_*\mathcal O_{D'}\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Rd''_*\mathcal O_{D''} \longrightarrow f_*(Rc'_*\mathcal O_{C'}\mathbin{\overset L\otimes} Rc''_*\mathcal O_{C''}) \]
(adjoint of the tensor product of the "base change" arrows \(f^*Rd'_*\mathcal O_{D'}\longrightarrow Rc'_*\mathcal O_{C'}\), \(f^*Rd''_*\mathcal O_{D''}\longrightarrow Rc''_*\mathcal O_{C''}\)).
The proof is essentially the same as that of 4.4; we leave to the reader the minor modifications to be made.

When \(c'\), \(c''\) are tor-independent, as well as \(d'\), \(d''\), the arrow (4) of 6.7.1 can be rewritten in the form \(f_*c_*K_C\longrightarrow d_*K_D\); it is simply defined by the adjunction arrow \(g_*K_C=g_*g^!K_D\longrightarrow K_D\) (like the arrow (4) of 4.4.1). It would however not be natural to consider only this case, for we would thereby exclude correspondences with excess intersection.
From 6.7 we deduce formulas analogous to 4.5.1, 4.7.1: under the hypotheses of 6.7, we have, for \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}(c_1'^*L_1,c_2'^!L_2)\), \(v\in\operatorname{Hom}(c_2''^*L_2,c_1''^!L_1)\),
\[ \langle f_*u,f_*v\rangle=f_*\langle u,v\rangle, \tag{6.7.2} \]
where \(f_*u\) (resp. \(f_*v\)) denotes the direct image correspondence of \(u\) (resp. \(v\)) in the sense of 6.5.5, and \(f_*\) on the right-hand side is the homomorphism

\[ \operatorname{Hom}(Rc'_*\mathcal O_{C'}\mathbin{\overset L\otimes} Rc''_*\mathcal O_{C''},K_X)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(Rd'_*\mathcal O_{D'}\mathbin{\overset L\otimes} Rd''_*\mathcal O_{D''},K_Y) \tag{6.7.3} \]

composite of the duality isomorphism 6.1.1 and of the arrow induced by the canonical arrow \(Rd'_*\mathcal O_{D'}\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Rd''_*\mathcal O_{D''} \longrightarrow f_*(Rc'_*\mathcal O_{C'}\mathbin{\overset L\otimes} Rc''_*\mathcal O_{C''})\) cited in 6.7. When \(Y_1=Y_2=S=D'=D''\), 6.7.2 is the Lefschetz-Verdier formula.
By way of illustration, we will show that this formula implies the Woods Hole formula [1].

6.8 For this we will need some reminders on the "Hodge" cohomology class associated with a regularly embedded subscheme of a smooth scheme and the Grothendieck residues ([8] III 9). Let \(f=X\longrightarrow S\) be a smooth morphism purely of relative dimension \(N\), \(i:Y\longrightarrow X\) a regular immersion of codimension \(d\) (i.e. \((SGA IV 16.9.2), (SGA 6 VII 1)\) a closed subscheme defined locally by a regular sequence of \(d\) equations, or, in an equivalent terminology, \((8 III 1), (SGA 6 VIII 1)\) a closed immersion which is (locally) a complete intersection of codimension \(d\)). Set \(g=fi\). We then associate with \(Y\) an element
\[ cl_{X/S}^{\ell}(Y)\in\operatorname{Ext}_{\mathcal O_X}^d (\mathcal O_Y,\Omega_{X/S}^d), \tag{6.8.1} \]
called the cohomology class associated with \(Y\), defined in the following manner. By the "purity" theorem ([8] III 7.2), we have

\[ \operatorname{Ext}_{\mathcal O_X}^i(\mathcal O_Y,\Omega_{X/S}^d)= \begin{cases} 0&\text{if }i\ne d,\\ \operatorname{Hom}(\bigwedge^dN_{Y/X},\Omega_{X/S}^d\otimes\mathcal O_Y)& \text{if }i=d, \end{cases} \tag{6.8.2} \]
where \(N_{Y/X}=I/I^2\) is the conormal sheaf \((I=\operatorname{Ker} (\mathcal O_X\longrightarrow\mathcal O_Y))\). Consequently,
\[ \operatorname{Ext}_{\mathcal O_X}^d(\mathcal O_Y,\Omega_{X/S}^d) =H^0(Y,\operatorname{Hom}(\bigwedge^dN_{Y/X},\Omega_{X/S}^d\otimes\mathcal O_Y)). \tag{6.8.3} \]
The differential \(d_{X/S}:\mathcal O_X\longrightarrow\Omega^1_{X/S}\) induces a \(\mathcal O_Y\)-linear map
\[ d_{X/S}\otimes1:N_{Y/X}\longrightarrow\Omega^1_{X/S}\otimes\mathcal O_Y. \]

The \(d\)-th exterior power of this map is a global section, over \(Y\), of \(\bigwedge^dN^\vee_{Y/S}\otimes\Omega^d_{X/S}\), whose image in \(\operatorname{Ext}^d_{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_Y,\Omega^d_{X/S})\) under the isomorphism 6.8.3 is by definition the class 6.8.1.
Suppose \(g\) finite and flat (hence \(d=N\)). Let \(\omega\in H^0(Y,\bigwedge^dN^\vee_{Y/S}\otimes\Omega^d_{X/S})\). By 6.8.2 and 6.1, we can view \(\omega\) as a homomorphism
\[ \mathcal O_Y\longrightarrow g^!\mathcal O_S (=\bigwedge^dN^\vee_{Y/S}\otimes\Omega^d_{X/S}), \]

hence, by adjunction, as a homomorphism \(g_*\mathcal O_Y\longrightarrow\mathcal O_S\). We define the residue \(relatif à (g)\) of \(\omega\) as the section of \(\mathcal O_S\) which is the image of 1 under this homomorphism, and we denote it \(\operatorname{Res}_{Y/S}(\omega)\) (cf. ([8] III 9)). On the other hand, let us denote by \(i_*\omega\in H^N(X,\Omega^N_{X/S})\) the image of \(\omega\) under the composite of the isomorphism 6.8.3 and of the canonical arrow
\[ \operatorname{Ext}^N_{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_Y,\Omega^N_{X/S}) \longrightarrow H^N(X,\Omega^N_{X/S}). \]
When \(f\) is proper, it follows from the transitivity for the trace morphisms (or adjunction arrows) \(i_*i^!\longrightarrow1\), \(f_*f^!\longrightarrow1\), that we have
\[ \operatorname{Res}_{Y/S}(\omega)=\operatorname{Tr}_f(i_*\omega), \tag{6.8.4} \]

where \(\operatorname{Tr}_f:H^N(X,\Omega^N_{X/S})\longrightarrow H^0(S,\mathcal O_S)\) is defined by the adjunction arrow \(f_*f^!\mathcal O_S=Rf_*\Omega^N_{X/S}[N]\longrightarrow\mathcal O_S\) (6.1). On the other hand, without any properness hypothesis, we have the important compatibility ([8] III 9 R6)
\[ \operatorname{Res}_{Y/S}(y\,cl^\ell_{X/S}(Y))=\operatorname{Tr}_{Y/S}(y), \tag{6.8.5} \]

for \(y\in H^0(Y,\mathcal O_Y)\), where \(\operatorname{Tr}_{Y/S}:g_*\mathcal O_Y\longrightarrow\mathcal O_S\) denotes the trace in the classical sense, i.e. \(\operatorname{Tr}_{Y/S}(y)\) = trace of the endomorphism of \(g_*\mathcal O_Y\) defined by multiplication by \(y\) (this compatibility is not proved in (loc. cit.); the case \(N=1\) is treated in [Raynaud, Anneaux locaux henséliens, VII 1, Lecture Notes in Math. nº169, Springer Verlag, 1970]). In other words, \(\operatorname{Tr}_{Y/S}\) corresponds, by adjunction, to the homomorphism \(\mathcal O_Y\longrightarrow g^!\mathcal O_S\) defined by multiplication by \(cl^\ell_{X/S}(Y)\). It follows that, for \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D(S)_{\rm coh}\), the arrow
\[ g^*F\longrightarrow g^!F=g^*F\otimes g^!\mathcal O_S \tag{6.8.6} \]
given by the product by \(cl^\ell_{X/S}(Y)\) corresponds by adjunction to the arrow
\[ g_*g^*F=F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}g_*\mathcal O_Y\longrightarrow F \tag{6.8.7} \]
defined by \(\operatorname{Tr}_{Y/S}:g_*\mathcal O_Y\longrightarrow\mathcal O_S\).

6.9 Let \(X\) (resp. \(Y\)) be a proper and smooth \(S\)-scheme of relative dimension \(m\) (resp. \(n\)), \(Z=X\times_SY\), \(a:A\longrightarrow Z\), \(b:B\longrightarrow Z\) regular immersions such that \(a_2:A\longrightarrow Y\), \(b_1:B\longrightarrow X\) are finite and flat (we denote by \(p_1:Z\longrightarrow X\), \(p_2:Z\longrightarrow Y\) the projections, and \(a_i=p_ia\), \(b_i=p_ib\)), \(c:C=A\times_ZB\longrightarrow Z\), \(L\in\operatorname{ob}D(X)_{\rm parf}\), \(M\in\operatorname{ob}D(Y)_{\rm parf}\) (the index parf means "perfect in the absolute sense", which is also equivalent to perfect rel. to \(S\), since \(X\) and \(Y\) are smooth). We assume \(C\) finite and flat over \(S\). The hypotheses made therefore imply that \(c\) is a regular immersion and that \(a\) and \(b\) are tor-independent.
We have \(p_1^*\Omega^1_{X/S}=\Omega^1_{Z/Y}\), \(p_2^*\Omega^1_{Y/S}=\Omega^1_{Z/X}\), and a decomposition
\[ \Omega^1_{Z/S}=\Omega^1_{Z/Y}\oplus\Omega^1_{Z/X}, \]
whence, for every integer \(r\), a decomposition
\[ \Omega^r_{Z/S}=\bigoplus_{i+j=r}\Omega^i_{Z/Y}\otimes\Omega^j_{Z/X}. \tag{6.9.0} \]
On the other hand, by 6.1.2, we have \(p_2^!M=p_2^*M\otimes\Omega^m_{Z/Y}[m]\), hence
\[ H^0(Z,a_*a^!R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(p_1^*L,p_2^!M)) =\operatorname{Ext}^m_{\mathcal O_Z} (a_1^*L,a_2^*M\otimes\Omega^m_{Z/Y}), \]
whence, by 6.4.4 and 6.8.2, an isomorphism
\[ \operatorname{Hom}(a_1^*L,a_2^!M)=H^0(A, R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(a_1^*L,a_2^*M)\otimes \bigwedge^mN^\vee_{A/Z}\otimes\Omega^m_{Z/Y}). \tag{6.9.1} \]
We similarly have an isomorphism
\[ \operatorname{Hom}(b_2^*M,b_1^!L)=H^0(B, R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(b_2^*M,b_1^*L)\otimes \bigwedge^nN^\vee_{B/Z}\otimes\Omega^n_{Z/X}). \tag{6.9.2} \]
Let \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}(a_1^*L,a_2^!M)\), \(v\in\operatorname{Hom}(b_2^*M,b_1^!L)\), and let us denote by
\[ \langle u,v\rangle\in H^0(C,\mathcal O_C), \tag{6.9.3} \]

the section \(\operatorname{Tr}(u_C\circ v_C)=\operatorname{Tr}(v_C\circ u_C)\), where \(u_C\in\operatorname{Hom}(c_1^*L,c_2^*M)\) and \(v_C\in\operatorname{Hom}(c_2^*M,c_1^*L)\) are induced by \(u\) and \(v\). Consider the cohomological correspondence \(u'\in\operatorname{Hom}(a_1^*L,a_2^!M)\) (resp. \(v'\in\operatorname{Hom}(b_2^*M,b_1^!L)\)) deduced from \(u\) (resp. \(v\)) by composition with the trace homomorphism 6.8.7 \(a_{2*}a_2^*M\longrightarrow M\) (resp. \(b_{1*}b_1^*L\longrightarrow L\)), or, what amounts to the same thing (6.8.6), by product with \(cl^\ell_{Z/Y}(A)\) (resp. \(cl^\ell_{Z/X}(B)\)). Let us note in passing that \(cl^\ell_{Z/Y}(A)\) (resp. \(cl^\ell_{Z/X}(B)\)) is the component in
\[ H^0(A,\bigwedge^mN^\vee_{A/Z}\otimes\Omega^m_{Z/Y}) \quad(\text{resp. }H^0(B,\bigwedge^nN^\vee_{B/Z}\otimes\Omega^n_{Z/X})) \]

of \(cl^\ell_{Z/S}(A)\) (resp. \(cl^\ell_{Z/S}(B)\)) defined by the projection \(\Omega^m_{Z/S}\longrightarrow\Omega^m_{Z/Y}\) (resp. \(\Omega^n_{Z/S}\longrightarrow\Omega^n_{Z/X}\)) of 6.9.0. We verify easily that the cup-product
\[ \langle u',v'\rangle\in \operatorname{Ext}^{m+n}_{\mathcal O_Z}(\mathcal O_C,\Omega^{m+n}_{Z/S}) =H^0(C,\bigwedge^{m+n}N^\vee_{C/Z}\otimes\Omega^{m+n}_{Z/S}) \]
defined in 6.6.3 is given by
\[ \langle u',v'\rangle=\langle u,v\rangle cl^\ell_{Z/Y}(A)cl^\ell_{Z/X}(B). \tag{6.9.4} \]
We verify on the other hand that the homomorphism 6.7.3
\[ \operatorname{Ext}^{m+n}_{\mathcal O_Z}(\mathcal O_C,\Omega^{m+n}_{Z/S}) \longrightarrow H^0(S,\mathcal O_S) \]
is the composite of the canonical arrow
\[ \operatorname{Ext}^{m+n}_{\mathcal O_Z}(\mathcal O_C,\Omega^{m+n}_{Z/S}) \longrightarrow H^{m+n}(Z,\Omega^{m+n}_{Z/S}) \]
and of the trace morphism \(\operatorname{Tr}_{Z/S}:H^{m+n}(Z,\Omega^{m+n}_{Z/S})\longrightarrow H^0(S,\mathcal O_S)\). The Lefschetz-Verdier formula 6.7.2 therefore yields the relation
\[ \langle(u')_*,(v')_*\rangle= \operatorname{Tr}_{Z/S}(\langle u,v\rangle cl^\ell_{Z/Y}(A)cl^\ell_{Z/X}(B)). \tag{6.9.5} \]
Thanks to 6.8.4 we can rewrite the right-hand side as a residue, so that in summary we have obtained:

Theorem 6.10 – Under the hypotheses of the first paragraph of 6.9, let \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}(a_1^*L,a_2^*M)\), \(v\in\operatorname{Hom}(b_2^*M,b_1^*L)\), and consider the cohomological correspondence \(u'\) (resp. \(v'\)) from \(L\) to \(M\) (resp. \(M\) to \(L\)) with support in \(A\) (resp. \(B\)) deduced from \(u\) (resp. \(v\)) by composition with the trace \(a_{2*}a_2^*M\longrightarrow M\) (resp. \(b_{1*}b_1^*L\longrightarrow L\)) (6.8.7), and the homomorphism \((u')_*:f_*L \longrightarrow g_*M\) (resp. \((v')_*:g_*M\longrightarrow f_*L\)) deduced from \(u'\) (resp. \(v'\)) by direct image (6.5.5) (\(f:X\longrightarrow S\), \(g:Y\longrightarrow S\)

denoting the projections). Then, with the notations of 6.8, 6.9.3, we have

\[ \langle(u')_*,(v')_*\rangle= \operatorname{Res}_{C/S}(\langle u,v\rangle cl^\ell_{Z/Y}(A)cl^\ell_{Z/X}(B)). \tag{6.10.1} \]

Remarks 6.11. – a) We verify as in 3.6, with the help of the description 3.5 b) of the direct image of a correspondence, that, if \(h:Z\longrightarrow S\) is the projection, \((u')_*\) (resp. \((v')_*\)) is the composite of the arrows

\[ f_*L\xrightarrow{(1)}(ha)_*a_1^*L\xrightarrow{(ha)_*u}(ha)_*a_2^*M =g_*a_{2*}a_2^*M\xrightarrow{(2)}g_*M \] \[ (\text{resp. }g_*M\xrightarrow{(1)}(hb)_*b_2^*M\xrightarrow{(hb)_*v} (hb)_*b_1^*L=f_*b_{1*}b_1^*L\xrightarrow{(2)}f_*L), \]

where (1) is the usual functoriality arrow (defined by the adjunction arrow \(1\longrightarrow a_{1*}a_1^*\) (resp. \(1\longrightarrow b_{2*}b_2^*\))) and (2) is given by the trace \(a_{2*}a_2^*M\longrightarrow M\) (resp. \(b_{1*}b_1^*L\longrightarrow L\)) (6.8.7).

b) Suppose that \(S\) is the spectrum of a field. The intersection \(C=A\times_{(X\times_SY)}B\) is therefore formed of isolated points. At each point \(z\) of \(C\), let us choose, near the images of \(z\), regular sequences \((s_1,\ldots,s_m)\), \((t_1,\ldots,t_n)\) of equations for \(A,B\). Then 6.10.1 can be written, with the notation of the residue symbols of ([8] III 9),
\[ \langle(u')_*,(v')_*\rangle=\sum_{z\in C}\operatorname{Res}_z\left( \langle u,v\rangle\prod_{1\leq i\leq m}(d_{Z/Y}s_i/s_i) \prod_{1\leq j\leq n}(d_{Z/X}t_j/t_j)\right), \tag{6.11.1} \]
where \(d_{Z/Y}s_i\) (resp. \(d_{Z/X}t_j\)) denotes the component of type (1,0) (resp. (0,1)) of \(d_{Z/S}s_i\) (resp. \(d_{Z/S}t_j\)) in the decomposition 6.9.0.

Corollary 6.12 – (Woods-Hole formula [1]). We assume that \(S\) is the spectrum of a field. Let \(X\) be a proper and smooth \(S\)-scheme, \(F\) an \(S\)-endomorphism of \(X\), \(L\) an object of \(D(X)_{\rm parf}\) (i.e. a perfect complex on \(X\)), \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}(F^*L,L)\). We assume that the scheme of fixed points \(X^F\) is finite and formed of transversal points (i.e. where the graph of \(F\) meets the diagonal transversally). Then we have

\[ \sum_i(-1)^i\operatorname{Tr}((F,u),H^i(X,L)) =\sum_{x\in X^F}\operatorname{Tr}(u_x)/\det(1-dF_x), \]

where \((F,u)\) is the endomorphism of \(H^i(X,L)\) composite of \(H^i(X,L)\longrightarrow H^i(X,F^*L)\)

(inverse image) and of \(H^i(X,u)\), \(\operatorname{Tr}(u_x)\) denotes the value at \(x\) of the endomorphism induced by \(u\) on the stalk of \(L\) at \(x\), and \(\det(1-dF_x)\) the value at \(x\) of the determinant of the map (1 − map induced by \(F\) on \(\Omega^1_{X,x}\)).

Proof. We apply 6.10 taking into account 6.11 a), b). Set \(Z=X\times_SX\). Let \(x\in X^F\). Near \(x\), let us choose a system of local coordinates \((x_1,\ldots,x_n)\) (defining an étale morphism into \(\mathbb A^n_S\)), set \(F_i=x_iF\), and denote by \((x_1,\ldots,x_n,y_1,\ldots,y_n)\) the corresponding local coordinates on \(Z\) near \(z=(x,x)\) \((x_i=x_i\otimes1), (y_i=1\otimes x_i)\). Then, near \(z\), \((x_1-y_1,\ldots,x_n-y_n)\) (resp. \((y_1-F_1,\ldots,y_n-F_n)\)) is a regular system of equations for the diagonal \(A\) (resp. the graph \(B\) of \(F\)). The contribution of the right-hand side of 6.11.1 at \(z\) is
\[ \langle u,1\rangle_z=\operatorname{Res}_z\left(\operatorname{Tr}(u) \frac{dx_1\cdots dx_n\,dy_1\cdots dy_n} {(x_1-y_1)\cdots(x_n-y_n)(y_1-F_1)\cdots(y_n-F_n)}\right). \]
Note that in the numerator we may replace \(dy_i\) by
\[ dy_i-dF_i=dy_i-\sum_{1\leq j\leq n}(dF_i/dx_j)dx_j. \]

Applying then the formula ([8] III 9 R3) to the situation \((B\longrightarrow Z\longrightarrow S)\), \((s_1,\ldots,s_p)=(y_1-F_1,\ldots,y_n-F_n)\), \((t_1,\ldots,t_n)=(x_1-y_1,\ldots,x_n-y_n)\), \(\omega=(\operatorname{Tr}(u)\,dx_1\cdots dx_n)\), we obtain
\[ (*)\qquad \langle u,1\rangle_z=\operatorname{Res}_x\left( \operatorname{Tr}(u)\frac{dx_1\cdots dx_n} {(x_1-F_1)\cdots(x_n-F_n)}\right). \]
Since \(x\) is transversal, the \(x_i-F_i\) form a regular system of parameters of \(\mathcal O_{X,x}\), so we can write, near \(x\),
\[ x_i=\sum_j(x_j-F_j)c_{ij}, \]

where \((c_{ij})\) is a matrix of sections of \(\mathcal O_X\) invertible at \(x\), its determinant having, at \(x\), the value \(\det(1-dF_x)^{-1}\). Thanks to ([8] III 9 R1) we therefore deduce from (*)
\[ \langle u,1\rangle_z=\operatorname{Res}_x\left( \operatorname{Tr}(u)\det(c_{ij})\frac{dx_1\cdots dx_n}{x_1\cdots x_n}\right), \]
whence, by ([8] III 9 R6),
\[ \langle u,1\rangle_z=\operatorname{Tr}(u(x))\det(c_{ij}(x)) =\operatorname{Tr}(u(x))\det(1-dF_x)^{-1}. \]
The corollary therefore follows from 6.11.1.
Remark 6.12.1. – If \(X^F\) is assumed only finite, the preceding computation shows that we have
\[ \sum(-1)^i\operatorname{Tr}((F,u),H^i(X,L))= \sum_{x\in X^F}\operatorname{Res}_x\left(\operatorname{Tr}(u) \frac{dx_1\cdots dx_n}{(x_1-F_1)\cdots(x_n-F_n)}\right), \]
where \((x_i)\) is a system of local coordinates at \(x\), and the \(F_i\) are the coordinates of \(F\) (near \(x\)) in this system.
The reader will convince himself, on the other hand, that the preceding formula is still valid if \(X\) is proper and assumed smooth only at the points of \(X^F\).

6.13 Suppose that \(S\) is the spectrum of an algebraic closure of the finite field \(\mathbb F_q\) and that \(X/S\) comes by extension of scalars from a proper and smooth scheme \(X_0/\mathbb F_q\). Take for \(F\) the Frobenius endomorphism of \(X/S\) "defined by raising to the \(q\)-th power on the coordinates". We have \(X^F=X_0(\mathbb F_q)\) and \(X^F\) is formed of transversal points since \(dF=0\). If \(L\) is a locally free sheaf of finite type on \(X\) and \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}(F^*L,L)\), the Woods-Hole formula therefore reads
\[ \sum(-1)^i\operatorname{Tr}((F,u),H^i(X,L))= \sum_{x\in X_0(\mathbb F_q)}\operatorname{Tr}(u_x,L_x). \tag{6.13.1} \]
In particular, for \(L=\mathcal O_X\), \(u\) the canonical isomorphism \(F^*\mathcal O_X\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathcal O_X\), we find:
\[ \sum(-1)^i\operatorname{Tr}(F,H^i(X,\mathcal O_X)) =\operatorname{Card}(X_0(\mathbb F_q))\pmod p. \tag{6.13.2} \]

Artin-Schreier theory allows us to rewrite the left-hand side in the form \(\sum(-1)^i\operatorname{Tr}(F,H^i(X,\mathbb F_p))\), where \(H^*(X,\mathbb F_p)\) denotes the étale cohomology of \(X\) with values in the constant sheaf \(\mathbb F_p\). By a refinement of this argument, Deligne, in (SGA 41/2 Fonctions L), deduced from 6.13.1 the following generalization of 6.13.2:

Theorem 6.13.3 (Deligne) – Let \(X_0\) be a scheme separated and of finite type over \(\mathbb F_q\), \(X\) the scheme deduced from \(X_0\) by extension of scalars to an algebraic closure \(k\) of \(\mathbb F_q\), \(F\) the Frobenius endomorphism of \(X/k\). Then, for every \(L_0\in\operatorname{ob}D_{ctf}(X_0,\mathbb F_p)\), we have, denoting by \(L\) the inverse image of \(L_0\) on \(X\),

\[ \sum(-1)^i\operatorname{Tr}(F,H_c^i(X,L)) =\sum_{x\in X^F}\operatorname{Tr}(F,L_x). \]

Let us point out that the special case of 6.13.3 corresponding to \(L_0=\mathbb F_p\) was established by Katz in (SGA 7 XXII) by an entirely different method, based on the Dwork-style computation of the zeta function of a hypersurface. Moreover, 6.13.2 in the proper and smooth case is also an immediate consequence of the Lefschetz formula in crystalline cohomology ([5] VII 3.1.9). In fact, crystalline cohomology gives the more precise information that each factor \(\det(1-Ft,H^i(X_0/W))\in W[t]\) of the zeta function is congruent mod \(p\) to \(\det(1-Ft,H^i(X_0,\mathcal O_{X_0}))\) (at least if \(H^*(X_0/W)\) is torsion-free). Katz showed [11] that one could obtain, with the help of the estimates of B. Mazur [14], higher congruences (involving the \(H^i(X_0,\Omega^j_{X_0})\) and the Cartier operation) for smooth hypersurfaces (or complete intersections) whose Hodge numbers (in the relevant dimension) are assumed to vanish up to a certain level.

On the other hand, if \(X_0\) is a proper scheme over \(\mathbb F_q\), geometrically integral, and such that \(H^i(X_0,\mathcal O_{X_0})=0\) for \(i>0\), the Lefschetz-Verdier formula 6.7.2, applied to \(X=X_0\otimes k\) and the Frobenius endomorphism of \(X\), immediately implies that \(X_0\) possesses at least one rational point over \(\mathbb F_q\) (since \(\operatorname{Tr}(F,R\Gamma(X,\mathcal O_X))=1\)). Serre asks whether this conclusion is still true when one replaces \(\mathbb F_q\) by a field satisfying \((C_1)\) ([17] II 3), for example \(\mathbb C(t)\) (Tsen), or \(\mathbb C((t))\) (Lang) (see loc. cit. for other examples); he observes indeed that, if \(X_0\) is a complete intersection of multi-degree \((m_1,\ldots,m_r)\) in \(\mathbb P^n\), the condition of triviality of the cohomology is equivalent ([16] n° 78) to the condition \(m_1+\cdots+m_r<n+1\), which is also the one that comes in when one writes the condition \((C_1)\).
6.14 The Woods Hole formula has numerous other applications, for which we refer to [2] and to the exposé of Beauville [4].

6.15 The writer has not examined the case of components of fixed points of dimension \(\geq1\), but it is not excluded that one could deduce from 6.7.2 (probably combined with Riemann-Roch) Lefschetz-Riemann-Roch formulas in the style of those of Donovan [7], Iversen [10], Nielsen [15] (at least with values in the base field). It is not excluded, on the other hand, that formula 6.7.2, applied over the dual numbers, yields residue formulas for vector fields analogous to those of Bott [6], [3].
